options.A: The fire area exceeds 12,000 sq ft, the fire area is located more than three stories above grade plane, or the combined area of all M fire areas on all floors exceeds 24,000 sq ft
options.B: Only when the building exceeds four stories
options.C: Only when the occupant load exceeds 1,000

explanation: IBC 903.2.7 requires sprinklers in a Group M occupancy where a fire area exceeds 12,000 sq ft, where a fire area is located more than three stories above grade plane, or where the combined area of all Group M fire areas on all floors, including any mezzanines, exceeds 24,000 sq ft. The 12,000 / 24,000 sq ft thresholds parallel the Group B, F-1, and S-1 triggers in Chapter 9.
verifier_note: Confirmed Group M sprinkler thresholds 12,000 sf fire area / >3 stories / 24,000 sf aggregate in IBC 903.2.7.

question: IBC Section 903.2.8 requires an automatic sprinkler system throughout all buildings containing a Group R fire area. Which statement best describes this requirement?

options.A: It applies only to R-1 hotels over four stories
options.B: It applies to all Group R occupancies (R-1, R-2, R-3, R-4) regardless of fire area size
options.C: It applies only when the fire area exceeds 12,000 sq ft
options.D: It applies only to R-2 apartment buildings

explanation: IBC 903.2.8 mandates an automatic sprinkler system installed throughout all buildings with a Group R fire area, with no minimum area threshold. The system type can vary, NFPA 13 for general R use, NFPA 13R for R occupancies up to four stories in height (per 903.3.1.2), or NFPA 13D for one- and two-family dwellings and townhouses, but the sprinkler requirement itself applies to all Group R.
verifier_note: Confirmed all Group R fire areas require sprinklers per IBC 903.2.8 (no area threshold).

question: An NFPA 13R sprinkler system, referenced in IBC Section 903.3.1.2, is permitted in Group R occupancies that meet which height limitation?

options.A: Up to and including four stories above grade plane and not exceeding 60 feet in height measured to the highest occupiable floor

explanation: IBC 903.3.1.2 permits an NFPA 13R system in Group R occupancies up to and including four stories above grade plane, with a maximum height of 60 feet measured to the highest occupiable floor. Above those limits a full NFPA 13 system is required. NFPA 13D (option D) is the standard for one- and two-family dwellings and townhouses, a different system.
verifier_note: Confirmed NFPA 13R limit of 4 stories above grade plane / 60 ft to highest occupiable floor in IBC 2018/2021 903.3.1.2.

question: Which sprinkler standard does IBC Section 903.3.1.3 reference for the protection of one- and two-family dwellings and townhouses?
explanation: IBC 903.3.1.3 references NFPA 13D for automatic sprinkler systems in one- and two-family dwellings and townhouses. NFPA 13 (option A) is the full commercial standard, NFPA 13R (B) is for low-rise residential up to four stories, and NFPA 25 (D) is the standard for the inspection, testing, and maintenance of water-based fire protection systems, not their design.
verifier_note: Confirmed NFPA 13D referenced for 1- and 2-family dwellings/townhouses in IBC 903.3.1.3.

question: Per IBC Section 903.4, where a required automatic sprinkler system serves a building, the valves controlling the water supply and other monitored components must be:

options.A: Automatically shut off the building electrical service when water flows
options.B: Electrically supervised, and water flow must transmit an alarm signal and activate occupant notification appliances
options.C: Equipped only with a local bell with no monitoring required
options.D: Inspected only once every five years

explanation: IBC 903.4 requires that valves controlling the water supply and other components be electrically supervised, and 903.4.2 requires an approved audible alarm/water-flow device. Activation of a required sprinkler system must transmit an alarm signal and activate alarm-notification appliances. This supervision ensures that valve closure (tamper) or water flow is detected and reported.
verifier_note: Confirmed IBC 903.4 monitoring/supervision and 903.4.2 alarm requirements for sprinkler systems.

question: IBC Section 905 and NFPA 14 define standpipe classes by intended user. A Class I standpipe system is intended for use by whom and provides what hose connection size?

options.A: Trained building occupants, with 1-1/2 inch connections
options.B: Fire department personnel and trained users, with 2-1/2 inch hose connections
options.C: Automatic fire suppression only, with no hose connection

explanation: Under IBC 905 / NFPA 14, a Class I standpipe provides 2-1/2 inch hose connections for use by fire department personnel or trained personnel with heavy fire-fighting hose streams. Class II provides 1-1/2 inch connections (option A) for use by the building occupants until the fire department arrives. Class III provides both 1-1/2 inch and 2-1/2 inch connections, serving both groups.
verifier_note: Confirmed Class I = 2-1/2 in fire-department connection, Class II = 1-1/2 in occupant use, Class III = both, per IBC 905 / NFPA 14.

question: Per IBC Section 905.3.1, a standpipe system is generally required in a building where the floor level of the highest story is located more than what distance above the lowest level of fire department vehicle access?
explanation: IBC 905.3.1 requires standpipe systems in buildings where the floor level of the highest story is more than 30 feet above the lowest level of fire department vehicle access, or where the floor level of the lowest story is more than 30 feet below the highest level of fire department vehicle access. (The 75-foot threshold, option B, is the separate definition of a high-rise building under Sections 202/403, which triggers additional protection.)
verifier_note: Confirmed standpipe primary trigger 30 ft above/below fire department vehicle access per IBC 905.3.1; 75 ft is the distinct high-rise definition (202/403).

question: IBC Section 907.2 establishes where manual and/or automatic fire alarm systems are required. A manual fire alarm system is generally NOT required (pull stations may be omitted) in a Group A occupancy when which condition is satisfied?

options.A: Never; A occupancies always require manual pull stations regardless
options.B: The building has an approved automatic sprinkler system per 903.3.1.1 and the occupant notification appliances activate upon sprinkler water flow
options.C: Only when the building is single-story
options.D: When the building is naturally ventilated

explanation: IBC 907.2.1 requires a manual fire alarm in Group A with an occupant load of 300 or more, or where the occupant load is more than 100 above or below the lowest level of exit discharge. Manual pull stations are not required where the building is equipped throughout with an automatic sprinkler system installed per 903.3.1.1 and the occupant notification appliances activate upon sprinkler water flow (907.2.1 exception).
verifier_note: Confirmed Group A manual fire alarm trigger (OL 300, or 100 above/below exit discharge) and sprinkler/notification exception in IBC 907.2.1.

question: Under IBC Section 907.5.2.3, where occupant notification is required, visible alarm notification appliances (strobes) must be provided in which areas to comply with accessibility requirements?

options.B: Public-use and common-use areas, in accordance with NFPA 72
options.D: Visible appliances are optional in all cases

explanation: IBC 907.5.2.3 requires visible alarm notification appliances (strobe lights) in public-use and common-use areas, and 907.5.2.3.1/.3.2 address public and common areas plus employee work areas, all in accordance with NFPA 72. This ensures notification reaches occupants with hearing impairments, an ADA/accessibility-driven requirement.
verifier_note: Confirmed visible notification in public/common areas per IBC 907.5.2.3 and NFPA 72.

question: Per IBC Section 907.2.3, in a Group E (educational) occupancy, manual pull stations may be limited to a single location when which conditions are met?

options.A: The occupant load is below 50, regardless of any other condition
options.B: The building is equipped throughout with an approved automatic sprinkler system, the notification appliances activate on sprinkler water flow, and manual activation is provided at a normally occupied location such as the main office
options.C: The school has fewer than 10 classrooms
options.D: The building is single-story and naturally ventilated

explanation: IBC 907.2.3 requires a manual fire alarm in Group E occupancies (exception for very small E with OL under 50). The pull-station exception allows manual stations to be limited to a single, constantly attended/normally occupied location such as the main office when the building has an automatic sprinkler system and the alarm notification activates automatically on water flow. This balances life safety with reduced false alarms in schools.
verifier_note: Confirmed Group E manual fire alarm + pull-station exception (sprinklered + single constantly-attended location) in IBC 907.2.3.

question: IBC Section 909 governs smoke control systems. When a smoke control system is provided to comply with the code, what is required to verify its proper operation before occupancy?

options.A: No testing; visual inspection of ducts only
options.B: Special inspections and acceptance testing in accordance with Sections 909 and 1705, of components such as fans, dampers, controls, and pressure differences

explanation: IBC 909 requires smoke control systems to be designed using a rational analysis (909.4), and 909.18 plus Section 1705.18 mandate special inspections and acceptance testing of the smoke control system components (fans, dampers, controls, pressure differences) before a certificate of occupancy is issued. The system must be commissioned and demonstrated to perform as designed.
verifier_note: Confirmed smoke control special inspection/acceptance testing requirement per IBC 909.18 / 1705.18.

question: Per IBC Section 906, the selection, installation, and distribution of portable fire extinguishers must conform to which standard?
explanation: IBC 906.2 requires that the selection, installation, and distribution of portable fire extinguishers comply with the International Fire Code and NFPA 10 (Standard for Portable Fire Extinguishers). NFPA 13 (A) is sprinklers, NFPA 72 (C) is fire alarm/signaling, and NFPA 14 (D) is standpipes.
verifier_note: Confirmed portable fire extinguishers per IBC 906 reference NFPA 10.

question: Under NFPA 10 as referenced by IBC Section 906, what is the maximum travel distance to a portable fire extinguisher for a Class A hazard (ordinary combustibles)?
explanation: NFPA 10 (referenced by IBC 906.3) establishes a maximum travel distance of 75 feet to a Class A fire extinguisher. For Class B (flammable liquids) the maximum travel distance is typically 30 or 50 feet depending on hazard level, and Class C/D/K have their own placement rules. IBC 906.3 reproduces these distance limits in its tables.
verifier_note: Confirmed 75 ft max travel distance to Class A extinguisher per NFPA 10 / IBC 906.3.

question: Where a fire pump is required to provide adequate water pressure or flow for a fire protection system, IBC Chapter 9 requires the fire pump installation to comply with which NFPA standard?
explanation: Fire pumps are designed and installed in accordance with NFPA 20 (Standard for the Installation of Stationary Pumps for Fire Protection), referenced through IBC Chapter 9 (e.g., 913). NFPA 25 (B) covers inspection/testing/maintenance, NFPA 13D (C) is residential sprinklers, and NFPA 70 (D) is the National Electrical Code. IBC 913 also requires fire pump rooms to be protected with specific fire-resistance separation.
verifier_note: Confirmed fire pumps per IBC 913 reference NFPA 20.

question: Per IBC Section 911, a fire command center is required for high-rise buildings and certain other structures. Which describes the minimum required size and rating of the fire command center?

options.A: No minimum; it can be any closet
options.B: A minimum of 200 square feet with a minimum dimension of 10 feet, separated from the remainder of the building by not less than a 1-hour fire barrier
options.C: A minimum of 50 square feet with no separation

explanation: IBC 911.1.2 requires the fire command center to be a minimum of 200 square feet in area with a minimum dimension of 10 feet, and 911.1.1 requires separation from the remainder of the building by not less than a 1-hour fire barrier. The room houses controls and indicators (annunciators, sprinkler/standpipe valves, smoke control panel, emergency power, etc.) for fire department use.
verifier_note: Confirmed fire command center min 200 sf, 10 ft min dimension, 1-hour fire barrier per IBC 911.1.1/.1.2.

question: IBC Section 903.2.9 requires sprinkler protection in Group S-1 storage occupancies. Which of the following correctly states a trigger?

options.A: Storage of office paper records, in any quantity
options.B: A Group S-1 fire area exceeding 12,000 sq ft, a fire area more than three stories above grade plane, a combined S-1 area on all floors exceeding 24,000 sq ft, plus specific repair-garage and high-piled-storage triggers

explanation: IBC 903.2.9 requires sprinklers in a Group S-1 fire area exceeding 12,000 sq ft, where a fire area is on a floor more than three stories above grade, or where the combined S-1 fire area on all floors exceeds 24,000 sq ft. 903.2.9.1 specifically addresses repair garages (by area, stories, and basement), and high-piled combustible storage (903.2.9.2 / IFC Chapter 32) carries additional sprinkler triggers based on commodity class and pile height.
verifier_note: Confirmed S-1 sprinkler triggers and repair garage/high-piled storage provisions in IBC 903.2.9 and subsections.

question: When an automatic sprinkler system is installed throughout a building, IBC permits various code reductions (sprinkler trade-offs). Which of the following is a recognized trade-off?

options.B: Increases in allowable building height and area (Tables 504 and 506) and increases in maximum exit access travel distance under Section 1017
options.C: Removal of all fire-resistance-rated assemblies in every case
options.D: Elimination of the requirement for a certificate of occupancy

explanation: A fully sprinklered building is permitted increases in allowable height (Table 504.3/504.4) and allowable area (Table 506.2 area factor for S1/SM vs NS), and longer maximum exit access travel distances under Table 1017.2. Sprinklers do not eliminate exits, all required fire-resistance assemblies, or the CO. These trade-offs reflect the proven life-safety value of sprinklers.
verifier_note: Confirmed sprinkler trade-offs for height (504), area (506), and travel distance (Table 1017.2) in IBC 2018/2021.

question: Per IBC Section 1010.1.10, panic hardware or fire exit hardware is required on latched/locked egress doors serving which occupancies?

options.B: Group A and Group E occupancies with an occupant load of 50 or more, and all Group H occupancies regardless of occupant load

explanation: IBC 1010.1.10 requires panic hardware (or fire exit hardware on fire-rated doors) on doors equipped with a latch or lock that serve a Group A or E occupancy with an occupant load of 50 or more, and on all Group H occupancies regardless of occupant load. The hardware must release the latch when a force of not more than 15 pounds is applied in the direction of egress travel.
verifier_note: Confirmed panic hardware required for A and E (OL 50+) and all H per IBC 2018/2021 1010.1.10.

question: Under IBC Section 1010.1.9, egress doors must be readily openable from the egress side without which of the following?

options.A: Without the use of a key, special knowledge, or effort

explanation: IBC 1010.1.9.1 requires that egress doors be openable from the egress side without the use of a key, special knowledge, or effort. The unlatching of any door must be accomplished by not more than one releasing operation (1010.1.9.5), and operable hardware must be mounted between 34 and 48 inches above the floor. This guarantees occupants can exit quickly under emergency conditions.
verifier_note: Confirmed egress-side operation without key/special knowledge/effort and single-operation unlatching per IBC 1010.1.9.

question: IBC Section 1010.1.9.9 permits sensor-release of electrically locked egress doors. Which condition is required for such locking arrangements?

options.A: The lock may stay engaged during a power failure
options.B: Loss of power to the sensor or locking device must automatically unlock the door in the direction of egress, and a manual unlocking device must be provided
options.C: Doors may be chained during occupancy

explanation: IBC 1010.1.9.9 (sensor release of electrically locked egress doors) requires that the egress-side sensor unlock the door upon detection of an approaching occupant, that loss of power to the sensor or locking device automatically unlock the door, and that a manual unlocking device (push button) be provided that, when operated, directly interrupts power for at least 30 seconds. Doors must fail unlocked, never locked, on power loss.
verifier_note: Confirmed sensor-release electrically locked egress door must fail unlocked on power loss and provide manual release per IBC 1010.1.9.9 (2018/2021).

question: Per IBC Section 1013.5, the word EXIT on an exit sign must use letters of what minimum height with what minimum principal stroke width?

options.A: Letters not less than 4 inches high with a stroke not less than 0.5 inch
options.B: Letters not less than 6 inches high with a principal stroke not less than 0.75 inch
options.C: Letters not less than 6 inches high with a stroke not less than 0.5 inch
options.D: Letters not less than 2 inches high

explanation: IBC 1013.5 requires exit sign graphics with the word EXIT in letters not less than 6 inches high, with the principal strokes of the letters not less than 0.75 inch wide, and minimum spacing between letters not less than 3/8 inch. Signs larger than minimum must keep stroke width proportional. The 6-inch letter / 3/4-inch stroke pairing is the standard answer.
verifier_note: Confirmed exit sign letters 6 in high, principal stroke 0.75 in, 3/8 in spacing per IBC 1013.5.

question: IBC Section 1008.2.1 requires the means of egress to be illuminated to a minimum level at the walking surface during occupancy. What is that minimum?
explanation: IBC 1008.2.1 requires the means of egress to be illuminated to a level of not less than 1 foot-candle (11 lux) at the walking surface during occupancy. Under emergency power conditions (1008.3), illumination may decline to an average of not less than 1 foot-candle initially and not less than 0.1 foot-candle minimum at the end of 90 minutes along the path of egress.
verifier_note: Confirmed 1 foot-candle minimum egress illumination (1008.2.1) and emergency power decline to 0.1 fc at 90 min (1008.3).

question: Per IBC Section 1011.5.2, the maximum riser height and minimum tread depth for means-of-egress (commercial) stairways are which of the following?

options.A: Maximum 8-1/4 inch riser, minimum 9 inch tread
options.B: Maximum 7 inch riser, minimum 11 inch tread
options.C: Maximum 7-3/4 inch riser, minimum 10 inch tread
options.D: Maximum 6 inch riser, minimum 13 inch tread

explanation: IBC 1011.5.2 establishes a maximum riser height of 7 inches and a minimum tread depth of 11 inches for means-of-egress stairways (the standard commercial 7/11 rule). This contrasts with the dwelling-unit limits of 7-3/4 inch maximum riser and 10 inch minimum tread, which the IBC allows only for stairs within individual dwelling units under the 1011.5.2 exceptions.
verifier_note: Confirmed commercial stair 7 in max riser / 11 in min tread per IBC 1011.5.2; dwelling-unit exception 7-3/4 / 10.

question: IBC Section 1011.2 sets the minimum width of stairways in the means of egress. What is the minimum clear width generally required where the stairway serves an occupant load of 50 or more?
explanation: IBC 1011.2 requires a minimum stairway width of 44 inches where the stairway serves an occupant load of 50 or more. Stairways serving an occupant load of less than 50 are permitted a minimum width of 36 inches. The required width is also subject to the per-occupant capacity factor of Section 1005 (0.3 inch per occupant for sprinklered, 0.2 inch otherwise), whichever yields the greater width.
verifier_note: Confirmed 44 in min stair width for OL 50+, 36 in for OL<50 per IBC 1011.2; capacity factor per 1005.

question: Per IBC Section 1011.8, what is the maximum vertical rise permitted for a single stairway flight between floor levels or landings?
explanation: IBC 1011.8 limits the maximum vertical rise of a stairway flight to 12 feet between floor levels or landings. A landing must be provided at the top and bottom of each stairway, and intermediate landings break flights so that no single flight rises more than 12 feet, reducing fall distance and providing rest points.
verifier_note: Confirmed 12 ft maximum vertical rise between landings per IBC 1011.8 (2018/2021).

question: IBC Section 1014.6 requires handrail extensions at stairway flights. At the top of a stair flight, the handrail must extend horizontally beyond the top riser nosing for what minimum distance?

options.B: Not less than 12 inches horizontally beyond the top riser nosing
options.D: No extension is required at the top

explanation: IBC 1014.6 requires the handrail at the top of a stairway to extend horizontally above the landing not less than 12 inches beyond the top riser nosing, and at the bottom to continue to slope for the depth of one tread beyond the bottom riser nosing. These extensions give a continuous graspable surface where users begin and finish the stair.
verifier_note: Confirmed handrail extension: top 12 in horizontal, bottom one tread depth beyond bottom nosing per IBC 1014.6.

question: Per IBC Section 1014.2, the top of handrail gripping surfaces must be mounted at what height above stair tread nosings and the finished surface of ramps?

options.C: Not less than 34 inches and not more than 38 inches

explanation: IBC 1014.2 requires handrail height, measured vertically from the top of the gripping surface, to be not less than 34 inches and not more than 38 inches above stair tread nosings and the finished surface of ramp slopes. A guard, by contrast, must be at least 42 inches high per 1015.3; do not confuse handrail height with guard height.
verifier_note: Confirmed handrail height 34-38 in per IBC 1014.2; distinguished from 42 in guard height (1015.3).

question: IBC Section 1019 regulates exit access stairways and ramps that are NOT within an interior exit stairway enclosure. Under the general rule, unenclosed (open) exit access stairways are permitted to connect how many stories?

options.A: Any number of stories with no limit
options.B: Not more than two stories, unless an express exception applies (e.g., mezzanines or certain sprinklered conditions per 1019.3)

explanation: IBC 1019.3 generally permits floor openings containing exit access stairways/ramps to connect not more than two stories without enclosure (including mezzanines and certain limited conditions). Connections of more than two stories require enclosure in a shaft (Section 713) or qualification as an interior exit stairway unless specific exceptions apply (atriums under 404, or limited sprinklered occupancy conditions). The two-story limit is the core rule.
verifier_note: Confirmed exit access stairways generally limited to connecting 2 stories unless enclosed/exception per IBC 1019.3.

question: Per IBC Section 1028.1, the exit discharge is the portion of the means of egress between the termination of an exit and a public way. What fraction of the number and capacity of exits is generally permitted to discharge through an interior element (such as a lobby) on the level of exit discharge?

options.B: Not more than 50 percent of the number and capacity of the exits
options.D: None; all exits must discharge directly to the exterior

explanation: IBC 1028.1 requires exits to discharge directly to the exterior/public way, but exception 1 (the lobby / level-of-exit-discharge exception) permits not more than 50 percent of the number and capacity of exits to discharge through an interior egress element such as a lobby or vestibule on the level of exit discharge, provided the level is sprinklered, the path is unobstructed and identifiable, and separation conditions are met.
verifier_note: Confirmed 50% exit discharge through lobby/level-of-discharge exception per IBC 1028.1, exception 1 (2018/2021).

question: IBC Section 1026 permits horizontal exits. What is the maximum percentage of the total required number of exits and exit capacity that horizontal exits are generally permitted to provide?

options.B: Not more than one-half (50 percent), except up to two-thirds in Group I-2 and up to 100 percent in Group I-3
options.D: Horizontal exits are not permitted in the IBC

explanation: IBC 1026.1 permits horizontal exits to comprise not more than one-half of the total number of exits or the total required exit minimum width/capacity from any building or floor. Exceptions allow horizontal exits to provide up to two-thirds in Group I-2 and up to 100 percent in Group I-3 (institutional/detention), where evacuation by relocation to an adjacent compartment is the design strategy.
verifier_note: Confirmed horizontal exits limited to 50% generally, 2/3 in I-2, 100% in I-3 per IBC 1026.1.

question: Per IBC Section 1026.2, the separation forming a horizontal exit (a fire wall or fire barrier) must have what minimum fire-resistance rating?
explanation: IBC 1026.2 requires that a horizontal exit be provided by a fire wall complying with Section 706 or, where the horizontal exit is on a single floor, a fire barrier complying with Section 707, in both cases having a fire-resistance rating of not less than 2 hours. The separation must be continuous and the area on each side must serve as a refuge area sized per 1026.4.
verifier_note: Confirmed horizontal exit separation min 2-hour fire wall/fire barrier per IBC 1026.2.

question: IBC Section 1029 governs assembly seating and aisles. For rows of seating, what is the minimum clear width of the aisle accessway between rows (the clear horizontal space between the back of one row and the nearest projection of the row behind)?
explanation: IBC 1029.13.2.1 requires a minimum clear width of 12 inches for the aisle accessway between rows of seating (measured as the clear horizontal distance from the back of the row ahead to the nearest projection of the row behind), increasing by 0.3 inch for each additional seat beyond a base number where the row exceeds the permitted seats. This guarantees passage to reach an aisle.
verifier_note: Confirmed 12 in minimum clear width for aisle accessway between rows per IBC 1029.13.2.1, plus 0.3 in/seat increase.

question: Per IBC Section 1029.14.2, aisle stairs in assembly seating may use risers steeper than ordinary stairs to accommodate sightlines. What is the maximum riser height permitted for assembly aisle stairs?

options.D: There is no maximum for assembly aisle stairs

explanation: IBC 1029.14.2 permits aisle stair risers a maximum of 9 inches and a minimum of 4 inches to accommodate sightlines in sloped seating. Within any flight the riser heights must be uniform, except that nonuniform risers required for sightlines are permitted only under controlled conditions with specific marking and lighting. The 9-inch maximum is the key figure that distinguishes assembly aisle stairs from the standard 7-inch egress stair riser.
verifier_note: Confirmed assembly aisle stair riser max 9 in / min 4 in per IBC 1029.14.2 (2018/2021), with uniformity/sightline conditions.

question: IBC Section 1006.2.1 limits the common path of egress travel. In a sprinklered Group B occupancy, what is the typical maximum common path of egress travel?
explanation: Per IBC Table 1006.2.1, the common path of egress travel for Group B (along with A, E, F, M, S, U) is limited to 75 feet, increased to 100 feet where the building is equipped throughout with an automatic sprinkler system and the occupant load of the space is within the table limits. The common path limit ensures occupants quickly reach a point with a choice of two exit directions.
verifier_note: Confirmed Group B common path 75 ft non-sprinklered / 100 ft sprinklered per IBC Table 1006.2.1 (2018/2021).

question: Per IBC Table 1006.2.1, a space is permitted a single exit/exit access doorway when the occupant load is within table limits. For Group B, F, M, S, and U occupancies, what is the maximum occupant load permitted with a single exit?
explanation: IBC Table 1006.2.1 sets the maximum occupant load for spaces with one exit/exit access doorway at 49 occupants for Group B, F, M, S, and U (and others), 29 for higher-hazard groups, and 10 for certain H occupancies, each subject to the listed maximum common path of travel. Above these occupant loads, at least two exits/exit access doorways are required.
verifier_note: Confirmed single-exit OL limit 49 for B/F/M/S/U, 29 and 10 for higher-hazard groups, per IBC Table 1006.2.1.

question: Per IBC Section 903.3.1.1, where a building is required to be equipped throughout with an automatic sprinkler system, which standard governs the design and how are small concealed spaces and closets treated?

options.A: NFPA 13; full coverage is required, and limited omissions (such as certain small closets or concealed spaces) are permitted only where NFPA 13 expressly allows
options.B: NFPA 13D; only living areas need coverage
options.D: No standard; the designer chooses freely

explanation: IBC 903.3.1.1 specifies that where sprinklers are required throughout, the system must be designed and installed in accordance with NFPA 13. Throughout means full coverage; specific limited omissions (e.g., small closets in dwelling units, certain electrical or concealed spaces) are permitted only where NFPA 13 itself expressly allows the omission. The designer cannot freely omit heads.
verifier_note: Confirmed NFPA 13 governs throughout systems per IBC 903.3.1.1; omissions only per NFPA 13 allowances.

question: Under IBC Section 403, a building is classified as a high-rise building requiring the special provisions of that section when it has an occupied floor located what distance above the lowest level of fire department vehicle access?
explanation: IBC defines a high-rise building (Section 202 / scoping in 403.1) as a building with an occupied floor more than 75 feet above the lowest level of fire department vehicle access. The 75-foot threshold triggers the additional protection requirements of Section 403, including increased sprinkler reliability, smokeproof exit enclosures, emergency power, and a fire command center.
verifier_note: 75-foot occupied-floor threshold confirmed for high-rise classification (IBC 2015/2021).

question: Per IBC Section 403.6.1, a fire service access elevator must be provided in high-rise buildings having an occupied floor more than what height above the lowest level of fire department vehicle access?
explanation: IBC 403.6.1 requires not fewer than two fire service access elevators (or all elevators, if fewer than two) where the building has an occupied floor more than 120 feet above the lowest level of fire department vehicle access. These elevators comply with Section 3007 and are intended to let firefighters reach upper floors without climbing stairs.
verifier_note: Fire service access elevator triggered at 120 ft occupied floor per 403.6.1 (IBC 2015).

question: In a high-rise building over 420 feet in building height, IBC Section 403 requires which of the following additional structural/fire measures beyond the baseline high-rise provisions?

options.A: A second, redundant fire pump and water supply (riser redundancy) and enhanced structural fire resistance
options.C: A reduction in required sprinkler coverage
options.D: Removal of the fire command center

explanation: For buildings exceeding 420 feet, IBC 403 adds requirements such as redundant water supply risers/fire pumps and increased fire-resistance ratings (structural frame protection). The 420-foot threshold reflects very tall buildings where additional redundancy is warranted; lower high-rises (over 75 feet) get the baseline package.
verifier_note: 420-foot threshold confirmed for riser redundancy and enhanced fire resistance in Section 403 (IBC 2015).

question: Under IBC Section 402, a covered mall building is limited to how many stories above grade plane and floor levels at any point (for the special mall provisions to apply)?
explanation: IBC 402.1 limits a covered mall building (and anchor buildings as governed) such that the mall does not exceed three floor levels at any point and is not more than three stories above grade plane. Beyond these limits the building cannot use the covered mall provisions and is regulated by the general code.
verifier_note: Three-story / three-floor-level limit confirmed in 402.1 (IBC 2015).

question: In a covered mall building, IBC Section 402 establishes a maximum travel distance within a tenant space to an exit or to the mall (which then serves as a pedestrian way) of how many feet?
explanation: IBC 402.8.5 permits a travel distance of up to 200 feet within the mall and tenant spaces in a covered mall building (a longer distance than the general occupancy travel limits) because the mall is treated as a controlled pedestrian way with smoke control and sprinklers throughout.
verifier_note: 200-foot travel distance confirmed for covered mall buildings, Section 402.8.5 (IBC 2015).

question: IBC Section 404 generally requires that an atrium be separated from adjacent spaces by a fire barrier of what minimum fire-resistance rating?
explanation: IBC 404.6 requires atrium spaces to be separated from adjacent spaces by a 1-hour fire barrier (or horizontal assembly). Exceptions allow a glass-wall smoke partition system or allow up to three floors to be open to the atrium without the barrier where the smoke control and sprinkler provisions of 404 are met.
verifier_note: 1-hour fire barrier for atrium separation confirmed, Section 404.6 (IBC 2015).

question: Which fire protection feature does IBC Section 404 require throughout a building containing an atrium?

options.B: An automatic sprinkler system throughout the building
options.C: Only manual fire alarm pull stations
options.D: No special systems beyond the base occupancy

explanation: IBC 404.3 requires an approved automatic sprinkler system throughout the entire building that contains an atrium (with a limited exception where the atrium ceiling is more than 55 feet above the floor). Section 404.5 also requires a smoke control system except where the atrium connects only two stories.
verifier_note: Building-wide sprinkler requirement confirmed for atriums, Section 404.3 (IBC 2015).

question: Per IBC Section 404, a smoke control system is required for an atrium EXCEPT when the atrium connects only how many stories?
explanation: IBC 404.5 requires an engineered smoke control system in accordance with Section 909 for atriums, with an exception where the atrium connects only two stories. Connecting three or more stories triggers the smoke control requirement.
verifier_note: Two-story smoke control exception confirmed, Section 404.5 (IBC 2015).

question: IBC Section 405 special provisions for underground buildings apply where the floor surface used for human occupancy is located how far below the lowest level of exit discharge?
explanation: IBC 405.1 applies the underground building provisions to building spaces with a floor used for human occupancy more than 30 feet below the lowest level of exit discharge. These spaces require compartmentation, smoke control, automatic sprinklers, and standby/emergency power.
verifier_note: 30-foot-below-exit-discharge threshold confirmed for underground buildings, Section 405.1 (IBC 2015).

question: Where an underground building under IBC Section 405 has occupied floor levels more than 30 feet below the lowest level of exit discharge, the code requires those levels to be divided into a minimum of how many smoke compartments?

options.B: Two compartments of approximately equal size

explanation: IBC 405.4.2 requires that the levels more than 30 feet below the lowest level of exit discharge be divided into a minimum of two smoke compartments of approximately equal size. The compartmentation lets occupants move horizontally to a protected area away from the fire.
verifier_note: Two-smoke-compartment compartmentation confirmed for underground buildings, Section 405.4.2 (IBC 2015).

question: Under IBC Section 406, an open parking garage is distinguished from an enclosed parking garage primarily by which characteristic?

options.B: It has uniformly distributed openings in the exterior walls for natural ventilation meeting the area requirements
options.C: It has fewer than 10 parking spaces

explanation: IBC 406.5 defines open parking garages by required natural ventilation: exterior openings distributed along at least two sides, with minimum aggregate opening area (generally not less than 20 percent of the total perimeter wall area, openings on not less than 40 percent of the perimeter). Enclosed garages (406.6) lack these openings and require mechanical ventilation per the mechanical code.
verifier_note: Open vs enclosed parking garage distinguished by natural ventilation openings, Section 406.5/406.6 (IBC 2015).

question: In a Group I-2 occupancy (such as a hospital or nursing home), IBC Section 407 requires that each smoke compartment containing sleeping rooms not exceed what maximum area?
explanation: IBC 407.5 requires Group I-2 occupancies to be divided into smoke compartments, each not exceeding 22,500 square feet, with travel distance to a smoke barrier door not exceeding 200 feet. This supports the defend-in-place strategy for patients who cannot self-evacuate.
verifier_note: 22,500 sq ft smoke compartment limit confirmed for Group I-2, Section 407.5 (IBC 2015).

question: IBC establishes five occupancy conditions for Group I-3 (detention and correctional). These conditions are based primarily on what?

options.B: The degree of restraint and the ability of occupants to evacuate (e.g., free movement vs. locked/restrained)
options.D: The presence of a sprinkler system

explanation: IBC 308.4 divides Group I-3 into Conditions 1 through 5 based on the degree to which occupants are restrained and their ability to respond to a fire and exit. Condition 1 allows free egress; Condition 5 has the highest restraint with remote-controlled locks. These conditions drive the locking, smoke compartment, and remote-release requirements of Section 408.
verifier_note: Group I-3 occupancy conditions based on restraint/egress ability confirmed, Section 308.4/408 (IBC 2015).

question: Under IBC Section 410, what is the dividing dimension that distinguishes a 'stage' from a 'platform' (which carries the proscenium and stage-specific requirements)?

options.A: Stage height (floor to roof/gridiron) greater than 50 feet
options.B: Stage area greater than 500 square feet
options.D: Any raised area more than 12 inches high

explanation: IBC 410.2 defines a stage by its height: where the space above the performance area exceeds 50 feet measured from the lowest point on the stage floor to the highest point of the roof or floor deck above. Stages over 50 feet require additional protection such as a proscenium wall and fire curtain (410.3.4); below that they may be regulated as platforms.
verifier_note: 50-foot stage height threshold confirmed in Section 410.2 (IBC 2015).

question: Per IBC Section 411, a special amusement building (such as a haunted house or enclosed dark ride) is required to have which feature regardless of occupant load?

options.B: An automatic sprinkler system and an automatic smoke detection system tied to the alarm/lighting

explanation: IBC 411 requires special amusement buildings, where the means of egress is not readily apparent or is intentionally confounded (low light, sound, special effects), to be equipped throughout with an automatic sprinkler system and an automatic smoke detection system. Actuation increases illumination and stops conflicting sounds/visual distractions so occupants can find exits.
verifier_note: Sprinkler + smoke detection requirement for special amusement buildings confirmed, Section 411.4/411.5 (IBC 2015).

question: Under IBC Section 415, Group H high-hazard occupancies storing or using hazardous materials in excess of the maximum allowable quantities (MAQ) are subdivided into how many H subdivisions?
explanation: Group H is divided into five subdivisions: H-1 (detonation hazard), H-2 (deflagration/accelerated burning), H-3 (readily supports combustion / physical hazard), H-4 (health hazards), and H-5 (semiconductor fabrication / HPM facilities). Section 415 provides the detailed construction, control area, and spill control requirements for each.
verifier_note: Five Group H subdivisions (H-1 through H-5) confirmed, Section 415/307 (IBC 2015).

question: IBC permits hazardous materials below the maximum allowable quantity (MAQ) to be stored in a non-H occupancy by dividing the building into 'control areas.' At the first story above grade, what is the maximum number of control areas permitted (before further reduction at upper stories)?
explanation: IBC Table 414.2.2 allows a maximum of four control areas per floor at the first story above grade plane, each holding up to the MAQ. The percentage of MAQ and the number of allowable control areas decrease at upper and lower stories (e.g., 75 percent / 3 areas at the second story), reflecting reduced firefighting access with height.
verifier_note: Four control areas maximum at first story above grade confirmed, Table 414.2.2 (IBC 2015).

question: IBC Section 416 (application of flammable finishes) applies to which type of operation?

options.B: Spray application of flammable or combustible finishing materials (spray booths and spray rooms)

explanation: IBC 416 regulates the spray application of flammable and combustible finishing materials, requiring spray rooms or spray booths constructed of noncombustible materials, with the required fire-resistance separation, ventilation, and interlocks. It addresses the high vapor/ignition risk of atomized finishes, not ordinary brush painting.
verifier_note: Section 416 scope is spray application of flammable finishes (spray booths/rooms), confirmed (IBC 2015).

question: Where a space is naturally ventilated under IBC Section 1203, the minimum openable area of openings to the outdoors must be not less than what percentage of the floor area being ventilated?
explanation: IBC 1203.5.1 requires the openable area of openings to the outdoors to be not less than 4 percent of the floor area being ventilated when natural ventilation is used. (Natural light, by contrast, is governed by a separate glazing-area requirement of 8 percent under Section 1205.)
verifier_note: 4 percent openable area for natural ventilation confirmed, Section 1203.5.1 (IBC 2015).

question: Under IBC Section 1204, interior occupied spaces served by a heating system must be capable of maintaining a minimum indoor temperature of what value, measured at a point 3 feet above the floor on the design heating day?
explanation: IBC 1204.1 requires interior spaces intended for human occupancy to be provided with heating capable of maintaining not less than 68 degrees F (20 degrees C) at a point 3 feet above the floor on the design heating day. Certain occupancies (F, H, S, U and process areas) are excepted.
verifier_note: 68 degrees F at 3 ft above floor confirmed, Section 1204.1 (IBC 2015).

question: IBC sound transmission provisions require walls, partitions, and floor-ceiling assemblies separating dwelling units (or sleeping units) from each other and from public/service areas to provide an airborne sound transmission class (STC) of not less than what value (laboratory tested)?
explanation: The interior environment chapter requires a minimum STC of 50 (or not less than 45 if field tested) for assemblies separating dwelling/sleeping units from adjacent units and public/service areas. In the IBC 2015 (and the Massachusetts 9th edition base) this is Section 1207.2; in the 2018/2021 IBC it is Section 1206.2. The companion impact insulation class (IIC) requirement is likewise 50 (45 field tested).
verifier_note: STC 50 (45 field) airborne sound confirmed, Section 1207.2 (IBC 2015); renumbered 1206.2 in later editions.

question: For structure-borne (impact) sound, IBC requires floor-ceiling assemblies separating dwelling units to provide an impact insulation class (IIC) of not less than what value when laboratory tested?
explanation: Floor-ceiling assemblies separating dwelling/sleeping units must achieve an IIC of not less than 50 (or 45 if field tested), matching the airborne STC 50 requirement. This is found at Section 1207.3 in the IBC 2015 (Massachusetts base) and renumbered to 1206.3 in the 2018/2021 IBC.
verifier_note: IIC 50 (45 field) impact insulation confirmed, Section 1207.3 (IBC 2015).

question: Per IBC Section 1208, occupiable spaces, habitable spaces, and corridors must have a minimum ceiling height of how much?
explanation: IBC 1208.2 requires a minimum ceiling height of 7 feet 6 inches for occupiable spaces, habitable spaces, and corridors. (Bathrooms, toilet rooms, kitchens, storage rooms, and laundry rooms may have a reduced 7-foot ceiling.) Habitable rooms other than kitchens must also be at least 7 feet in any plan dimension per 1208.1.
verifier_note: 7 ft 6 in ceiling height confirmed, Section 1208.2 (IBC 2015).

question: Under IBC Section 1210 (toilet and bathroom requirements), walls within 2 feet of the front and sides of urinals and water closets must have a smooth, hard, nonabsorbent surface to what minimum height above the floor?
explanation: IBC 1210.2.2 requires that walls within 2 feet of the front and sides of urinals and water closets have a smooth, hard, nonabsorbent surface to a height of not less than 4 feet above the floor, and that such surface be of a type not adversely affected by moisture. Floors must likewise be smooth, hard, and nonabsorbent (1210.2.1).
verifier_note: Nonabsorbent wall surface to 4 ft height confirmed, Section 1210.2.2 (IBC 2015).

question: IBC limits combustible trim (excluding handrails and guardrails) to what maximum percentage of the aggregate wall and ceiling area of any room or space?
explanation: Under the interior finish provisions, combustible trim, other than handrails and guardrails, is limited to not more than 10 percent of the aggregate wall and ceiling areas of any room or space. This caps the amount of lower-rated combustible material used as decorative trim. The interior finish material classification itself is governed by Chapter 8, while Chapter 12 governs the overall interior environment.
verifier_note: 10 percent combustible trim limit confirmed, Section 806.5 (IBC 2015).

question: Under IBC Section 3002, elevator hoistway enclosures must be constructed as shaft enclosures complying with which IBC section?
explanation: IBC 3002.1 requires elevator, dumbwaiter, and other hoistway enclosures to be constructed as shaft enclosures complying with Section 713. This generally means a 2-hour fire-resistance-rated enclosure connecting four or more stories and 1-hour for fewer stories, with rated hoistway doors.
verifier_note: Hoistway enclosures comply with Section 713 shaft enclosures, confirmed 3002.1 (IBC 2015).

question: IBC Section 3002.4 requires that buildings with how many or more stories above or below grade plane provide at least one elevator car sized to accommodate an ambulance stretcher 24 inches by 84 inches?
explanation: IBC 3002.4 requires buildings four or more stories above or below grade plane to have not fewer than one elevator able to accommodate an ambulance stretcher 24 inches by 84 inches in its horizontal open position, identified by the Star of Life symbol. This ensures emergency medical access to all served floors.
verifier_note: 4-or-more-story stretcher elevator (24 by 84 in) confirmed, Section 3002.4 (IBC 2015).

question: IBC Section 3007 requires the fire service access elevator lobby to have a minimum floor area of not less than 150 square feet, with a least dimension of not less than how many feet?
explanation: IBC 3007.6 requires each fire service access elevator lobby to be not less than 150 square feet in area with a minimum dimension of 8 feet. The lobby is enclosed by fire partitions and serves as a protected staging area for firefighters.
verifier_note: 150 sq ft / 8 ft minimum lobby dimension confirmed, Section 3007.6 (IBC 2015).

question: A fire service access elevator under IBC Section 3007 requires the building to be provided with what fire protection feature?

options.B: An automatic sprinkler system throughout (except where prohibited in hoistway and machine room)
options.D: Only portable fire extinguishers in the lobby

explanation: IBC 3007.2 requires the building be protected throughout by an approved automatic sprinkler system, except that sprinklers are not permitted in the fire service access elevator machine room, machinery space, control space, or hoistway (3007.2.1) to protect the elevator equipment. The water-protected occupancy plus a protected, smoke-resistant lobby is what makes the elevator usable for firefighting.
verifier_note: Building-wide sprinklers (excluded from FSAE machine room/hoistway) confirmed, Section 3007.2 (IBC 2015).

question: Where occupant evacuation elevators are provided under IBC Section 3008, they must be installed in relation to the required egress stairways in what way?

options.A: They replace the required exit stairways
options.B: They are provided in addition to the required number and capacity of exit stairways
options.C: They eliminate the need for an area of refuge

explanation: Occupant evacuation elevators (IBC 3008) are a permitted enhancement; they do not reduce the required number, capacity, or arrangement of exit stairways. The building must still provide fully compliant exit stairways, and the evacuation elevators supplement egress with protected lobbies, standby power, and water/smoke protection.
verifier_note: Occupant evacuation elevators do not reduce required exit stairways, confirmed Section 3008 (IBC 2018/2021; reserved in some 2015 printings).

question: Under IBC Chapter 30, an elevator machine room or machinery space generally must NOT be used for which purpose?

options.A: Housing the elevator controllers and drive equipment
options.B: Storage of building maintenance supplies or other non-elevator equipment/uses

explanation: IBC 3005.4 prohibits elevator machine rooms, machinery spaces, control rooms, and control spaces from being used for purposes other than for the elevator equipment; building storage and unrelated equipment are not permitted. The rooms must also be enclosed and have the required fire-resistance and access provisions.
verifier_note: Machine rooms restricted to elevator use only confirmed, Section 3005.4 (IBC 2015).

question: Under IBC Section 1104, an accessible route is required to connect which of the following?

options.A: Only the main entrance to the elevator
options.B: Accessible building or facility entrances with all accessible spaces and elements within the building or facility
options.D: Only floors served by an elevator

explanation: IBC 1104.1 requires accessible routes to connect accessible building/facility entrances with all accessible spaces and elements within the building or facility that are otherwise connected by a circulation path. Section 1104.4 requires that multistory buildings provide an accessible route to all stories (with limited exceptions for small buildings and certain non-occupied levels).
verifier_note: Accessible route connectivity scoping confirmed, Section 1104.1 (IBC 2015).

question: Per IBC Section 1105, at least what percentage of public entrances to a building must be accessible?
explanation: IBC 1105.1.1 requires that not fewer than 60 percent of all public entrances be accessible. Additional scoping in Section 1105 also requires accessible entrances where they serve parking, transportation facilities, tunnels/elevated walkways, and tenant spaces.
verifier_note: 60 percent accessible public entrances confirmed, Section 1105.1.1 (IBC 2015).

question: Under IBC Table 1106.1, a parking facility with a total of 1 to 25 spaces must provide a minimum of how many accessible parking spaces?
explanation: IBC Table 1106.1 requires 1 accessible space for lots of 1 to 25 total spaces, 2 for 26 to 50, 3 for 51 to 75, 4 for 76 to 100, and increasing thereafter. The accessible spaces must be on the shortest accessible route to an accessible entrance.
verifier_note: 1 accessible space for 1-25 total spaces confirmed, Table 1106.1 (IBC 2015).

question: IBC Section 1106 requires that at least one in every how many accessible parking spaces (but not fewer than one) be van accessible?
explanation: IBC 1106.5 requires that for every six or fraction of six accessible parking spaces, at least one must be a van-accessible space (served by the wider access aisle and adequate vertical clearance). At minimum, one van-accessible space is required wherever accessible parking is provided.
verifier_note: 1-in-6 van-accessible ratio confirmed, Section 1106.5 (IBC 2015).

question: Under IBC Section 1107, in a Group R-2 occupancy (e.g., apartments) containing more than 20 dwelling units, what is the minimum scoping for Accessible (Type A) units?

options.A: No Type A units are ever required
options.B: At least 2 percent, but not fewer than one, of the units must be Type A
options.C: 10 percent of the units must be Type A
options.D: All units must be Type A

explanation: IBC 1107.6.2.2.1 requires that in Group R-2 occupancies with more than 20 dwelling or sleeping units, at least 2 percent, but not fewer than one, be Type A units. The remaining units in buildings with an elevator (or ground-floor units without one) are generally required to be Type B units per 1107.6.2.2.2.
verifier_note: 2 percent (min one) Type A units for R-2 over 20 units confirmed, Section 1107.6.2.2.1 (IBC 2015).

question: Per IBC Section 1111, which entrances/elements must be identified by the International Symbol of Accessibility (ISA)?

options.B: Accessible parking spaces, accessible passenger loading zones, and accessible entrances when not all entrances are accessible

explanation: IBC 1111 requires the International Symbol of Accessibility at accessible parking spaces (1111.1), at accessible passenger loading zones, and at accessible entrances where not all entrances are accessible, plus directional signage at inaccessible entrances/toilet rooms pointing to the nearest accessible one. This guides users with disabilities to the compliant features.
verifier_note: ISA signage scoping confirmed, Section 1111 (IBC 2015).

question: Under IBC Section 3202, an awning projecting from a building wall over a public right-of-way and located less than 8 feet above grade must maintain a vertical clearance of not less than how many feet?
explanation: IBC 3202.2.3 permits awnings to project into the public right-of-way provided a minimum vertical clearance of 7 feet is maintained from the sidewalk. Below-8-foot encroachments are otherwise tightly limited (steps and architectural features generally to 12 inches) to keep the pedestrian way clear.
verifier_note: 7-foot minimum vertical clearance for awnings below 8 ft confirmed, Section 3202.2.3 (IBC 2015).

question: IBC Section 3202 limits below-grade structural footings of street walls (located not less than 8 feet below grade) to a maximum projection beyond the street lot line of how much?
explanation: IBC 3202.1 (Encroachments below grade) permits footings of street walls located not less than 8 feet below grade to project not more than 12 inches beyond the street lot line into the public right-of-way. This avoids conflicts with utilities and adjacent below-grade work.
verifier_note: 12-inch max footing projection below grade confirmed, Section 3202.1 (IBC 2015).

question: For encroachments above grade and 8 feet or more above the sidewalk under IBC Section 3202.3, windows, balconies, architectural features, and mechanical equipment may encroach how much for each additional 1 inch of clearance above 8 feet, up to a maximum encroachment?

options.D: No encroachment is permitted above 8 feet

explanation: IBC 3202.3.2 permits 1 inch of encroachment for each additional 1 inch of clearance above 8 feet, with the maximum encroachment limited to 4 feet. This sliding scale lets higher features project farther while preserving headroom and a clear pedestrian zone below.
verifier_note: 1 inch per inch above 8 ft, max 4 ft encroachment confirmed, Section 3202.3.2 (IBC 2015).

question: An above-grade pedestrian walkway (skybridge) that encroaches over a public right-of-way under IBC Section 3202.3 must maintain a minimum vertical clearance over the sidewalk/roadway of how many feet?
explanation: IBC 3202.3.4 requires pedestrian walkways over a public right-of-way to provide a minimum vertical clearance of 15 feet. Awnings, marquees, and similar features with less than 15 feet of clearance are limited to two-thirds of the sidewalk width, while encroachments 15 feet or more above grade are generally unrestricted by the projection rules.
verifier_note: 15-foot vertical clearance for pedestrian walkways confirmed, Section 3202.3.4 (IBC 2015).

question: Under IBC Section 3202.4, temporary encroachments (such as construction protection or seasonal features) into the public right-of-way are limited to a maximum projection of 3 feet or what fraction of the sidewalk width, whichever is less, while maintaining a minimum 7-foot clearance?
explanation: IBC 3202.4 limits temporary encroachments to a projection of 3 feet or one-fourth of the sidewalk width (whichever is less), maintaining at least 7 feet of clearance, and generally for the limited duration the building official approves. This keeps a usable pedestrian path during temporary work.
verifier_note: Temporary encroachment 3 ft or one-fourth sidewalk width, 7 ft clearance confirmed, Section 3202.4 (IBC 2015).

question: IBC Section 402 requires a covered mall building (and its anchor buildings, as applicable) to be equipped throughout with which system?

options.B: An automatic sprinkler system in accordance with Section 903.3.1.1
options.D: Smoke vents in lieu of sprinklers

explanation: IBC 402.5 requires the covered mall building, including the mall and all tenant spaces, to be protected throughout by an automatic sprinkler system installed per Section 903.3.1.1, with the system designed so a portion can be shut off without affecting the rest. Sprinklers are foundational to the relaxed travel distances and large open mall concept.
verifier_note: Building-wide NFPA 13 sprinklers required for covered mall buildings confirmed, Section 402.5 (IBC 2015).

question: In a Group I-2 occupancy, IBC Section 407 limits the travel distance from any point in a smoke compartment to a smoke barrier door to a maximum of how many feet?
explanation: IBC 407.5 limits travel distance from any point in a Group I-2 smoke compartment to the door in a smoke barrier to 200 feet. Combined with the 22,500-square-foot maximum compartment size, this ensures patients can be relocated horizontally to an adjacent compartment quickly under the defend-in-place strategy.
verifier_note: 200-foot travel distance to smoke barrier door in I-2 confirmed, Section 407.5 (IBC 2015).

question: Under IBC Chapter 30, escalator floor openings (that are not within an enclosed shaft) connecting more than the permitted number of stories must be protected by which means where required?

options.A: Left completely unprotected in all cases
options.B: An approved power-operated automatic shutter or by sprinkler/draft-curtain protection where permitted
options.C: A 4-hour fire wall in all cases
options.D: Only a smoke detector with no physical protection

explanation: IBC 3004 and the related shaft enclosure provisions of Sections 712/713 require escalator and moving walk floor openings connecting more than the permitted number of stories to be enclosed, or protected by approved methods such as a power-operated automatic shutter, or by sprinkler/draft-curtain protection where specifically permitted for the occupancy. The goal is to limit vertical smoke and fire spread through the open well.
verifier_note: Escalator opening protection (shutter or sprinkler/draft-curtain methods) confirmed, Section 3004/712 (IBC 2015).

question: In a commercial building, an egress door serving a room is required to swing in the direction of egress travel when the occupant load served by that door is at least how many people?
explanation: Side-hinged swinging, pivoted, and balanced egress doors must swing in the direction of egress travel where serving a room or area with an occupant load of 50 or more, or where serving any Group H occupancy. Below 50 occupants the door may swing either way.
verifier_note: Confirmed: occupant load of 50 or more (or Group H) triggers swing-in-direction-of-egress. 2021 IBC numbers it 1010.1.2.1; 50-person threshold stable across editions.

question: What is the minimum required ceiling height of a means of egress, measured above the finished floor?
explanation: The means of egress must have a ceiling height of not less than 7 feet 6 inches (2286 mm) above the finished floor. Protruding objects may extend below this where a minimum headroom of 80 inches is maintained over circulation paths, and no more than 50 percent of the ceiling area may be reduced by such objects.
verifier_note: Confirmed 7 ft 6 in across 2018, 2021 and 2024 IBC Section 1003.2. Distinct from the 80-inch (6 ft 8 in) minimum door height of 1010.1.1.

question: Under the IBC, the minimum height of an egress door opening is:
explanation: Egress doors must provide a minimum clear height of 80 inches (the door leaf is typically 6 feet 8 inches / 80 inches tall). The minimum clear width of a single egress door leaf is 32 inches. Do not confuse the 80-inch door height with the 7-foot-6-inch egress ceiling height.
verifier_note: Confirmed 80-inch minimum door height and 32-inch minimum clear width, IBC 1010.1.1.

question: A restaurant, tavern, or nightclub intended for the consumption of food and drink is classified under which IBC occupancy group?
explanation: Group A-2 covers assembly uses intended for food and/or drink consumption, including restaurants, cafeterias, banquet halls, taverns, bars, nightclubs, and casinos. A-1 is fixed-seating theaters, A-3 is worship/recreation/amusement, and Group B is business.
verifier_note: Confirmed A-2 = food/drink consumption assembly, Section 303.3 in 2021/2024 IBC.

question: An apartment building or non-transient boarding house where occupants are primarily permanent is classified as which residential occupancy group?
explanation: Group R-2 contains sleeping units or more than two dwelling units where occupants are primarily permanent, including apartment houses, dormitories, and non-transient boarding houses. R-1 is transient (hotels/motels), R-3 is one- and two-family permanent dwellings, and R-4 is residential care/assisted living for 6 to 16 persons.
verifier_note: Confirmed R-2 = apartments/permanent, Section 310.3 (2021/2024: R-1=310.2, R-2=310.3, R-3=310.4, R-4=310.5).

question: A residential occupancy that provides 24-hour custodial care for more than five but not more than 16 persons (such as an assisted-living group home) is classified as:
explanation: Group R-4 is a residential care/assisted-living facility housing more than five but not more than 16 persons (excluding staff) receiving custodial care. Facilities for five or fewer persons fall under R-3, and larger institutional care facilities are classified as Group I.
verifier_note: Confirmed R-4 = more than 5 but not more than 16 persons, custodial care, Section 310.5 (2021/2024).

question: A retail department store where merchandise is displayed and sold to the public is classified under which occupancy group?
explanation: Group M (Mercantile) covers buildings used for the display and sale of merchandise where the public has access, including department stores, retail stores, markets, and motor fuel-dispensing facilities. Group B is offices/professional services and Group S is storage.
verifier_note: Confirmed Group M mercantile = display and sale of merchandise, Section 309.1.

question: A high-hazard occupancy that involves materials presenting a detonation hazard, such as explosives, is classified as:
explanation: Group H-1 contains materials that present a detonation hazard (e.g., explosives, certain unstable reactives). H-2 = deflagration/accelerated burning hazard, H-3 = materials that readily support combustion, and H-4 = materials that are health hazards (corrosives, toxics).
verifier_note: Confirmed H-1 = detonation hazard, defined within Section 307.

question: In Type IV (Heavy Timber) construction, what is the minimum nominal dimension for solid sawn wood columns supporting floor loads?
explanation: In Heavy Timber (Type IV-HT) construction, wood columns supporting floor loads must be not less than 8 inches nominal in any dimension (8x8). Columns supporting roof loads only may be 6 inches nominal in the smaller dimension. These minimum sizes give heavy timber its inherent fire resistance.
verifier_note: Confirmed 8x8 nominal minimum for HT columns supporting floors (6 in. for roof-only). 2021 IBC split Type IV into IV-A/B/C/HT; the 8-inch HT column rule and 2304.11 heavy-timber sizing remain.

question: Which type of construction is characterized by exterior walls of noncombustible material and interior building elements of solid or laminated wood with no concealed spaces (Heavy Timber)?
explanation: Type IV (Heavy Timber, HT) requires noncombustible exterior walls and interior elements of solid or laminated wood without concealed spaces, meeting minimum member dimensions. Type III has noncombustible exterior walls with combustible interior elements; Type V permits combustible materials throughout.
verifier_note: Confirmed Type IV = Heavy Timber, noncombustible exterior walls + heavy timber interior, Section 602.4.

question: Which of the following work generally does NOT require a building permit under the IBC?

options.A: A one-story detached storage shed with a floor area of 120 square feet or less
options.B: A retaining wall 6 feet high measured from the bottom of the footing

explanation: Section 105.2 exempts one-story detached accessory structures (tool/storage sheds, playhouses) not exceeding 120 square feet of floor area. Fences not over 7 feet and retaining walls not over 4 feet (bottom of footing to top of wall, with no surcharge) are also exempt. A 6-foot retaining wall, a mezzanine, and removal of a load-bearing wall all require permits.
verifier_note: Confirmed: 120 sf detached accessory structure exempt; fence not over 7 ft and retaining wall not over 4 ft also exempt, IBC 105.2. Exemption does not authorize code-violating work.

question: Under the IBC, a fence (other than a swimming pool barrier) does NOT require a building permit when it is not over what height?
explanation: Section 105.2 exempts fences not over 7 feet high (excluding swimming pool barriers) from the permit requirement. Note the contrast with retaining walls, which are exempt only up to 4 feet measured from the bottom of the footing to the top of the wall.
verifier_note: Confirmed fence not over 7 ft exempt, IBC 105.2.

question: Under the IBC, when may a newly constructed building be lawfully occupied?

options.A: As soon as the building permit is issued
options.C: Only after the building official issues a certificate of occupancy
options.D: Whenever the contractor declares the work substantially complete

explanation: Section 111 states that a building or structure shall not be used or occupied (and no change of occupancy made) until the building official has issued a certificate of occupancy. The certificate follows successful completion of required inspections and verification of code compliance.
verifier_note: Confirmed IBC Section 111: occupancy prohibited until certificate of occupancy issued by building official.

question: When a building official issues a written stop work order because work is being performed in a manner that endangers life or property, what must occur?

options.A: The contractor may finish the current task before stopping
options.B: Cited work must immediately cease until the order is lifted
options.C: Work may continue if the owner posts a bond
options.D: The order only takes effect after a 72-hour notice period

explanation: Upon issuance of a stop work order, the cited work must immediately cease. The order is given in writing to the owner, the owner's agent, or the person doing the work, stating the reason and the conditions under which work may resume. Continuing work (except to correct the violation) subjects the person to fines.
verifier_note: Confirmed: cited work shall immediately cease (IBC 115.3). Stop work order is Section 115 in current IBC; some jurisdictions (e.g., Virginia) renumber to 114.

question: An automatic sprinkler system is required throughout a Group A-2 (food/drink assembly) fire area when the fire area exceeds what floor area?
explanation: Per Section 903.2.1.2, a sprinkler system is required throughout a Group A-2 occupancy where any of these apply: the fire area exceeds 5,000 square feet; the fire area has an occupant load of 100 or more; or the fire area is on a floor other than a level of exit discharge serving the occupancy.
verifier_note: Confirmed against UpCodes 903.2.1.2: 5,000 sf, occupant load 100+, or floor other than level of exit discharge. Some web summaries cite 3,000 sf erroneously; verified 5,000.

question: A Group A-2 fire area also requires an automatic sprinkler system when its occupant load reaches at least:
explanation: Section 903.2.1.2 requires sprinklers throughout a Group A-2 occupancy when the fire area has an occupant load of 100 or more (in addition to the 5,000-square-foot and exit-discharge-level triggers).
verifier_note: Confirmed occupant load of 100 or more triggers A-2 sprinklers, UpCodes 903.2.1.2.

question: A Class III standpipe system is generally required where the floor level of the highest story is located more than what distance above the lowest level of fire department vehicle access?
explanation: Section 905.3.1 requires standpipes where the highest story floor level is more than 30 feet above the lowest level of fire department vehicle access, or where the lowest floor level is more than 30 feet below the highest level of such access. In fully sprinklered buildings a Class I system is generally permitted (Exception 1).
verifier_note: Confirmed 30 ft above/below fire department vehicle access, IBC 905.3.1. The 75-foot figure is the high-rise threshold, a deliberate distractor.

question: Under the IBC, the maximum area of a single exterior standard-unit glass-block (glass unit masonry) panel, at a design wind pressure of 20 psf, is:
explanation: Section 2110.3 limits each individual exterior standard-unit glass-block panel to a maximum of 144 square feet at 20 psf design wind pressure, with maximum dimensions of 25 feet wide or 20 feet high between supports. Thin-unit exterior panels are limited to 85 square feet.
verifier_note: Confirmed 144 sf standard-unit exterior panel at 20 psf; 85 sf thin-unit; 25 ft wide / 20 ft high max, IBC 2110.3.

question: On a building of construction type other than Type I, a penthouse used to enclose mechanical equipment may not exceed what height above the roof deck?
explanation: Section 1510.2 limits penthouses on buildings other than Type I construction to 18 feet above the roof deck (measured to the average height of the penthouse roof). A higher limit of 28 feet is allowed where the penthouse encloses tanks or elevators that travel to the roof.
verifier_note: Confirmed 18 ft general / 28 ft for tank/elevator enclosures, IBC 1510.2 (rooftop structures, Chapter 15 in 2015+ IBC).

question: Under the IBC, the maximum vertical height (thickness) of a marquee, measured from its lowest to highest point, where it projects more than two-thirds of the distance from the lot line to the curb line, is:
explanation: Section 3106 limits a marquee's vertical thickness to 3 feet where it projects more than two-thirds of the distance from the lot line to the curb line, and to 9 feet where it projects less than two-thirds of that distance. The greater the projection over the public way, the thinner the marquee must be.
verifier_note: Confirmed 3 ft (projects >2/3 to curb) and 9 ft (projects <2/3), IBC 3106. Minimum vertical clearance above sidewalk is set by local ordinance, separate from this thickness rule.

question: Rooftop-mounted photovoltaic panel/module systems are required to be tested, listed, and identified with a fire classification in accordance with which standard?
explanation: Rooftop-mounted PV panels and modules must be tested, listed, and identified with a fire classification per UL 1703 (with mounting systems addressed by UL 2703). ASTM E84 is the surface burning (flame-spread) test for interior finishes, NFPA 70 is the National Electrical Code, and UL 217 covers smoke alarms.
verifier_note: Confirmed UL 1703 fire classification for rooftop PV (UL 2703 for mounting). Section number varies by edition; standard reference UL 1703 is stable.

question: A permit for a temporary structure (such as an event tent) under the IBC is limited to a maximum service period of:
explanation: Section 108 limits permits for temporary structures and uses to a maximum of 180 days, after which the building official may grant extensions for demonstrated cause. The IBC defines a 'temporary structure' as one erected for a period of 180 days or less.
verifier_note: Confirmed 180-day maximum, extensions for cause, IBC Section 108. Not Section 3103, which covers tent/membrane structural requirements.

question: Where natural light is provided by exterior glazed openings, the minimum net glazed area must be at least what percentage of the floor area of the room served?
explanation: Section 1204.2 requires a minimum net glazed area of 8 percent of the floor area of the room served for natural light. Note the contrast with natural ventilation, which requires an openable area of at least 4 percent of the floor area being ventilated.
verifier_note: Confirmed 8 percent net glazed area for natural light, IBC 1204.2.

question: Where natural ventilation is provided by openings to the outdoors, the minimum openable area must be at least what percentage of the floor area being ventilated?
explanation: The IBC (Section 1203.5 in 2021; 1203.4 in 2015/2018) requires that the minimum openable area to the outdoors be 4 percent of the floor area being ventilated for natural ventilation. The 8 percent figure applies to natural light glazing, not ventilation.
verifier_note: Confirmed 4 percent openable area for natural ventilation. Section renumbered across editions (1203.4.1 in 2015/2018, 1203.5.1 in 2021); value 4 percent is stable.

question: In the standard steel shape designation 'W8 x 15,' what does the number 15 represent?

options.A: The nominal depth of the member in inches
options.B: The weight of the member in pounds per linear foot
options.D: The cross-sectional area in square inches

explanation: In a W-shape (wide-flange) designation such as W8 x 15, the first number (8) is the nominal depth in inches and the second number (15) is the weight in pounds per linear foot. So a W8 x 15 is a wide-flange beam roughly 8 inches deep weighing 15 lb/ft.
code_reference: AISC Steel Construction Manual (referenced by IBC Chapter 22)
verifier_note: Standardized AISC nomenclature: W(depth) x (weight in lb/ft). Design/manual knowledge referenced by IBC Chapter 22 (Steel), not a numbered IBC section, but the convention is universal.

question: Under the IBC, the fire-resistance-rated separation (or sprinkler protection) for an incidental use such as a furnace room, an incinerator room, or a laundry room over 100 square feet is governed by which provision?
explanation: Incidental uses (areas posing a higher hazard within a larger occupancy, e.g., furnace rooms, incinerator rooms, paint shops, laundry rooms larger than 100 square feet) are separated from the remainder of the building per Section 509 and Table 509, which specify a fire-rated separation, an automatic sprinkler system, or both.
verifier_note: Confirmed incidental uses governed by IBC Section 509 and Table 509 (2015/2018/2021). Specific row values vary, so question is framed at the governing-section level to keep the gabarito airtight.

question: Under the 2021 IECC (R402.4.1.2), what is the maximum allowable building air leakage rate, by blower-door test, for a new dwelling in Massachusetts (Climate Zone 5)?

options.A: 5.0 air changes per hour at 50 Pa (5 ACH50)
options.B: 3.0 air changes per hour at 50 Pa (3 ACH50)
options.C: 7.0 air changes per hour at 50 Pa (7 ACH50)
options.D: 1.0 air change per hour at 50 Pa (1 ACH50)

explanation: The 2021 IECC R402.4.1.2 caps air leakage at 5.0 ACH50 in Climate Zones 0, 1 and 2, and at 3.0 ACH50 in Climate Zones 3 through 8. Massachusetts is Zone 5, so the limit is 3.0 ACH50. (The 2018 IECC had allowed 5 ACH50 in zones 3-4; the 2021 edition tightened it.) The test is run at 0.2 in. w.g. (50 Pascals).
code_reference: IECC R402.4.1.2 (2021) — Air leakage testing, CZ 3-8

question: The blower-door air leakage test required by IECC R402.4.1.2 must be conducted at what reference pressure?
explanation: IECC R402.4.1.2 requires the whole-house pressurization/depressurization test be performed at a pressure of 0.2 inch water gauge (50 Pascals), which is why the metric is expressed as 'ACH50' (air changes per hour at 50 Pa). Testing follows RESNET/ICC 380, ASTM E779, or ASTM E1827 and is done with a blower door.

question: Under the 2021 IECC postconstruction duct test (R403.3.6), what is the maximum total duct leakage allowed for a system with ducts located partly outside the building thermal envelope?

options.A: 8.0 cfm per 100 sq ft of conditioned floor area
options.B: 4.0 cfm per 100 sq ft of conditioned floor area
options.C: 12.0 cfm per 100 sq ft of conditioned floor area
options.D: 2.0 cfm per 100 sq ft of conditioned floor area

explanation: Per the 2021 IECC, the postconstruction total duct leakage limit is 4.0 cubic feet per minute per 100 square feet of conditioned floor area. A rough-in test with the air handler installed uses the same 4.0 cfm/100 sq ft limit (3.0 cfm/100 sq ft if the air handler is not yet installed).

question: Under the 2021 IECC (R403.3.5), duct leakage testing is NOT required when which of the following is true?

options.A: The ducts and air handler are located entirely within the building thermal envelope
options.B: The home is smaller than 2,000 square feet
options.C: The ductwork is flexible duct rather than rigid metal
options.D: The home uses a heat pump instead of a furnace
