explanation: R310.3.2.1 requires window wells with a vertical depth greater than 44 inches to be equipped with a permanently affixed ladder or steps usable with the window in the fully open position. The ladder/steps may project up to 6 inches into the well.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R310.3.2.1: ladder/steps required when well depth > 44 in; may project 6 in.

question: Per R310.3.2.1, the rungs of a window well ladder must have an inside width of at least how much and be spaced no more than how far apart vertically?

options.A: At least 12 inches wide, spaced not more than 18 inches on center
options.B: At least 9 inches wide, spaced not more than 24 inches on center
options.C: At least 16 inches wide, spaced not more than 12 inches on center
options.D: At least 6 inches wide, spaced not more than 30 inches on center

explanation: R310.3.2.1 specifies that ladders or rungs serving window wells must have an inside width of at least 12 inches, project at least 3 inches from the wall, and be spaced not more than 18 inches on center vertically for the full height of the window well.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R310.3.2.1: rungs >=12 in wide, project >=3 in, spaced <=18 in o.c.

question: Per R703.11, vinyl siding must be fastened so that what condition is met?

options.A: Fasteners driven tight to lock the panel in place
options.B: Fasteners centered in the slots and not driven tight, allowing for thermal movement
options.D: Fasteners spaced not more than 48 inches on center

explanation: R703.11.2 requires vinyl siding fasteners to penetrate the framing/substrate at least the required depth and to be centered in the nailing slots without being driven tightly, leaving approximately 1/32 inch (the thickness of a dime) between the fastener head and the siding so the panel can expand and contract.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R703.11.2: vinyl siding fasteners centered in slots, not driven tight (allow movement).

question: Per R703.3 and R703.4, exterior wall coverings such as fiber cement, wood and hardboard siding must be installed over what, in addition to the structure?

options.B: A water-resistive barrier, and to the minimum thickness/attachment listed in Table R703.3(1)
options.D: A vapor retarder only on the warm side

explanation: R703.1 and R703.4 require exterior wall coverings to be installed over a water-resistive barrier (R703.2) and attached in accordance with the minimum thickness and fastening requirements of Table R703.3(1) for the specific siding material, including fiber cement (R703.10), wood and hardboard siding.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R703.1/R703.3/R703.4: sidings over WRB, per Table R703.3(1) thickness/attachment.

question: Per R703.10.1, fiber-cement lap siding having a maximum width of 12 inches must be lapped a minimum of how much?
explanation: R703.10.1 requires fiber-cement lap siding (maximum 12-inch width) to be lapped a minimum of 1-1/4 inches and to have the ends sealed, covered with battens, or otherwise treated, and to be installed over a water-resistive barrier.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R703.10.1: fiber-cement lap siding lapped min 1-1/4 in.

question: Per R703.2, a water-resistive barrier behind the exterior wall covering must consist of at least what?

options.A: Nothing, if the siding is rated water-resistant
options.B: One layer of No. 15 asphalt felt (ASTM D226 Type 1) or other approved water-resistive barrier
options.D: A single coat of exterior paint

explanation: R703.2 requires not fewer than one layer of No. 15 asphalt felt, complying with ASTM D226 Type 1, or other approved water-resistive barrier to be applied over studs or sheathing of all exterior walls. The barrier must be applied horizontally with upper layers lapped over lower layers to shed water.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R703.2: min one layer No. 15 asphalt felt (ASTM D226 Type 1) or approved WRB.

question: Per R703.2, the water-resistive barrier must be applied in what manner to properly shed water?

options.B: Horizontally, with upper layers lapped over lower layers (shingle fashion)
options.C: In a single sheet with no laps
options.D: Behind the sheathing, against the studs

explanation: R703.2 requires the water-resistive barrier to be applied horizontally with the upper layer lapped over the lower layer (weatherboard / shingle fashion) so that any water reaching the barrier is directed down and out, away from the structure. It is applied over the sheathing, not behind it.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R703.2: WRB applied horizontally, upper over lower (shingle fashion).

question: Per R1003.18, the air space clearance between a masonry chimney located entirely outside the exterior walls and combustible material must be at least how much?
explanation: R1003.18 requires a minimum air space clearance to combustibles of 2 inches for chimneys located in the interior of the building, and 1 inch for chimneys located entirely outside the exterior walls (an exterior chimney). Combustible material must not be placed within these clearances.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R1003.18: 2 in clearance for interior chimney, 1 in for exterior chimney.

question: Per R1003.18, the required air-space clearance between a masonry chimney and combustible trim or framing may be reduced or eliminated only when what condition is met?

options.A: It is painted with fire-retardant paint
options.B: Noncombustible material is used at the interface, or the air space is otherwise preserved per the section
options.D: It is no thicker than 1 inch

explanation: R1003.18 maintains the required air-space clearance to combustibles, but allows combustible material such as exterior trim to be placed at the masonry chimney only where the air space is preserved or noncombustible material is used at the interface. The clearance space itself may not be filled with combustible material.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R1003.18: required air space to combustibles must be maintained; noncombustible interface allowed.

question: Per R602.7, headers and girders supporting floor and roof loads over openings in load-bearing walls must be sized according to what?

options.B: The span tables (Table R602.7) based on span, supported loads and ground snow load

explanation: Header and girder sizes over openings in load-bearing walls are determined from the girder/header span tables (Table R602.7(1)-(3)) based on the clear span of the opening, the building width / supported loads, and the ground snow load, ensuring the member can carry the tributary floor and roof loads.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R602.7 / Table R602.7(1)-(3): header/girder size by span, supported loads, ground snow load.

question: Per R404.2.3, the size, height and spacing of studs in a permanent wood foundation wall (and the thickness of supporting plywood) must be determined based on what?

options.B: The height of fill (backfill) and the applied lateral soil load
options.D: The number of floor joists only

explanation: R404.2.3 requires the size, height and spacing of studs and the thickness of the supporting plywood in a permanent wood foundation to be determined based on the height of fill (backfill) and the resulting lateral soil pressure, in accordance with the AWC permanent wood foundation design specification referenced by the code.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R404.2.3: wood foundation stud size/spacing and plywood thickness keyed to backfill height/lateral load.

question: Per R905.10.3, metal roof panels and shingles must be attached so they resist what primary load?

options.B: The component and cladding wind uplift pressures for the building
options.D: Live load from occupants on the roof

explanation: R905.10.3 requires metal roof panel and shingle attachment to resist the wind uplift (component and cladding) pressures determined for the building, in accordance with the manufacturer's listing and the wind provisions of the code. Wind uplift is the governing concern for metal roofing attachment.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R905.10.3: metal roofing attachment resists component and cladding wind uplift.

question: Per R407.3, columns must be restrained against what at the bottom end where they bear on a footing or pier?

options.B: Lateral displacement, and they must be protected against corrosion (steel) or decay (wood)

explanation: R407.3 requires columns to be restrained to prevent lateral displacement at the bottom end (and at the top where they support girders), typically by a connection to the footing/pier. Steel columns must also be protected against corrosion, and wood columns protected against decay where exposed.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R407.3: columns restrained against lateral displacement at base/top; corrosion/decay protection.

question: Per R310.3.2.1, the ladder or steps required for a deep window well are permitted to encroach into the required minimum well dimension by not more than how much?
explanation: R310.3.2.1 permits the permanently affixed ladder or steps required in window wells deeper than 44 inches to encroach a maximum of 6 inches into the required minimum horizontal dimension/area of the window well.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R310.3.2.1: ladder/steps may project up to 6 in into required well dimension.

question: Under Table R602.10.4, the bracing method designated 'WSP' refers to which of the following?
explanation: In Table R602.10.4, 'WSP' is the abbreviation for Wood Structural Panel bracing. Other recognized intermittent methods include LIB (let-in bracing), DWB (diagonal wood boards), GB (gypsum board), PBS (particleboard sheathing), and SFB (structural fiberboard sheathing).
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 Table R602.10.4: WSP = wood structural panel.

question: Per Table R602.10.4, the bracing method abbreviation 'LIB' identifies which method?

options.B: Let-in bracing (1x4 wood or metal strap let into studs)

explanation: 'LIB' in Table R602.10.4 stands for Let-In Bracing, which consists of a nominal 1-inch-by-4-inch wood let-in to the studs at an angle (45 to 60 degrees), or an approved metal strap installed in the same manner.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 Table R602.10.4: LIB = let-in bracing (1x4 or metal strap).

question: Under Section R602.10.4, the 'PFH' continuous method is what type of braced wall panel?
explanation: PFH stands for Portal Frame with Hold-downs, a continuous method (per R602.10.6.2) used to provide bracing capacity in narrow wall segments adjacent to garage door and other large openings where standard panel widths cannot be met. It uses tie-down devices and specific sheathing/fastening details.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R602.10.6.2: PFH = portal frame with hold-downs (method for narrow panels).

question: Per Table R602.10.1.2, what is the maximum spacing permitted between braced wall lines in most conventional light-frame construction?
explanation: Braced wall lines are generally required to be spaced not more than 25 feet on center, measured perpendicular to the braced wall line. Spacing up to 35 feet is permitted only under specific exception conditions with increased bracing amounts.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R602.10.1.2: braced wall line spacing generally 25 ft, up to 35 ft with exception.

question: Under Table R602.10.4, the minimum length of a braced wall panel using continuous wood structural panel sheathing (CS-WSP) is generally based on which factor?

options.A: A fixed 96 inches regardless of height
options.B: The adjacent clear opening height (with a ratio table)

explanation: For continuous sheathing methods (CS-WSP), the minimum braced wall panel length is determined from a table relating the panel length to the maximum adjacent clear opening height (height-to-length ratio), allowing shorter panels than the standard 48-inch intermittent minimum.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R602.10.5: CS panel length based on adjacent clear opening height ratio.

question: Per R311.7.5.2.1, winder treads must have a minimum tread depth of 6 inches at any point and not less than what depth at the walkline (12 inches from the narrow edge)?
explanation: R311.7.5.2.1 requires winder treads to have a minimum tread depth of 10 inches measured at the walkline, located 12 inches from the side where the treads are narrower. The minimum tread depth at any point is 6 inches.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R311.7.5.2.1: winder 10 in at walkline (12 in point), 6 in minimum at any point.

question: Under R311.7.10.1, a spiral stairway must have a clear walking width of at least 26 inches and a minimum tread depth of 6-3/4 inches at the walkline located what distance from the narrower edge?
explanation: R311.7.10.1 specifies spiral stairways with a minimum clear width of 26 inches at and below the handrail, treads having a depth of not less than 6-3/4 inches at the walkline (12 inches from the narrower edge), and a minimum headroom of 6 feet 6 inches. Riser height must not exceed 9-1/2 inches.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R311.7.10.1: spiral 26 in clear width, 6-3/4 in tread at walkline 12 in from narrow edge, max riser 9-1/2 in.

question: Per R311.7.2, the minimum headroom in all parts of a stairway must not be less than which dimension, measured vertically from the sloped line of tread nosings?
explanation: R311.7.2 requires a minimum stairway headroom of 6 feet 8 inches measured vertically from the sloped line adjoining the tread nosing or from the floor surface of the landing or platform.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R311.7.2: stairway headroom minimum 6 ft 8 in.

question: Per Table R301.5, guards and handrails must be designed to resist a single concentrated load of how many pounds applied in any direction along the top?
explanation: Table R301.5 establishes that guards and handrails must resist a concentrated load of 200 pounds applied in any direction at any point along the top. Guard in-fill components are separately designed for a 50-pound concentrated load on a 1-square-foot area.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 Table R301.5: guard/handrail concentrated load 200 lb any direction; in-fill 50 lb.

question: Per Table R403.1(1), for a 1-story dwelling with conventional light-frame construction on soil with a load-bearing value of 1,500 psf, the minimum footing width is generally what?
explanation: Table R403.1(1) provides minimum footing widths based on load-bearing value of the soil and number of stories. For 1-story light-frame construction on 1,500 psf soil, the minimum footing width is 12 inches. Wider footings are required for additional stories or lower-capacity soils.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 Table R403.1(1): 1-story light-frame on 1500 psf soil = 12 in footing width.

question: Per R403.1, the minimum thickness for a concrete footing supporting walls of light-frame construction is generally not less than what?
explanation: Section R403.1 (and Table R403.1) establish a minimum concrete footing thickness of 6 inches for typical conditions. The footing projection beyond the foundation wall and the soil bearing capacity also govern the required dimensions.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R403.1: minimum footing thickness 6 in.

question: Per R403.1.4, footings must be placed below the frost line or otherwise protected from frost. In Massachusetts, which has a deep design frost depth, this means footings are typically placed at what minimum depth?

options.B: Below the locally established frost line (commonly 48 inches in much of MA)
options.D: Frost protection is not required in MA

explanation: R403.1.4 requires footing bottoms to extend below the frost line of the locality or be protected by an approved method of frost protection (such as frost-protected shallow foundations per R403.3). Massachusetts adopts a deep frost line, commonly around 48 inches; the authority having jurisdiction sets the local depth.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R403.1.4: footings below local frost line or frost-protected; MA local depth commonly ~48 in (set by AHJ).

question: Per E3601 (and E3602), what is the minimum service rating required for a one-family dwelling?
explanation: Section E3602.1 requires that for a one-family dwelling, the service disconnecting means have a rating of not less than 100 amperes, 3-wire. This corresponds to the NEC 230.79(C) minimum service rating for dwellings.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 E3602.1: dwelling service minimum 100 A, 3-wire.

question: Per E3608.1.2, when a metal underground water pipe is used as a grounding electrode, it must be supplemented by an additional electrode and must be in direct contact with the earth for not less than how many feet?
explanation: E3608.1.2 requires that a metal underground water pipe in direct contact with the earth for 10 feet or more qualify as a grounding electrode, and it must be supplemented by an additional grounding electrode (such as a ground rod or concrete-encased electrode).
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 E3608.1.2: water pipe electrode requires 10 ft earth contact + supplemental electrode.

question: Per E3608.1.4, a ground rod electrode (rod and pipe type) must have a length in contact with the soil of not less than what?
explanation: E3608.1.4 specifies rod and pipe electrodes not less than 8 feet in length, installed so that at least 8 feet of length is in contact with the soil. The rod is generally driven to a depth of 8 feet, with the upper end flush with or below grade.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 E3608.1.4: ground rod minimum 8 ft, 8 ft in contact with soil.

question: Per Table E3705.1, a 20-ampere branch circuit using copper conductors generally requires what minimum conductor size?
explanation: Table E3705.1 (and the overcurrent protection rules) require 12 AWG copper for a 20-ampere branch circuit. 14 AWG copper is limited to 15-ampere circuits, and 10 AWG copper is used for 30-ampere circuits.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 E3705/Table E3705.1: 20 A circuit = 12 AWG copper; 15 A = 14 AWG; 30 A = 10 AWG.

question: Per Table E3905.12.2.1, what volume allowance (in cubic inches) is assigned to each 14 AWG conductor when calculating box fill?
explanation: Table E3905.12.2.1 assigns a free space volume of 2.00 cubic inches per 14 AWG conductor. For 12 AWG it is 2.25 cubic inches, and for 10 AWG it is 2.50 cubic inches. These allowances are used to verify the box is large enough for its conductors, devices, and fittings.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 Table E3905.12.2.1: 14 AWG = 2.00 cu in, 12 AWG = 2.25, 10 AWG = 2.50.

question: Per E3903.2, at least one wall switch-controlled lighting outlet must be installed in which of the following spaces?

options.B: Habitable rooms, bathrooms, hallways, stairways, and attached garages
options.C: Only rooms larger than 70 square feet
options.D: Only the living room and bedrooms

explanation: E3903.2 requires at least one wall switch-controlled lighting outlet in every habitable room and bathroom. E3903.3 and related sections extend lighting outlet requirements to hallways, stairways, attached garages, detached garages with power, and at exterior egress doors.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 E3903.2/E3903.3: switch-controlled lighting outlets in habitable rooms, baths, halls, stairs, garages.

question: Per E3902, GFCI protection is required for 125-volt, single-phase, 15- and 20-ampere receptacles in which of the following dwelling locations?

options.B: Bathrooms, garages, outdoors, crawl spaces, unfinished basements, kitchens, and within 6 feet of sinks

explanation: E3902 requires GFCI protection in a broad list of locations including bathrooms, garages and accessory buildings, outdoors, crawl spaces, unfinished basements, kitchen receptacles serving countertops, and receptacles within 6 feet of sinks, plus laundry areas, dishwashers, and others.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 E3902: GFCI in baths, garages, outdoors, crawl spaces, unfinished basements, kitchens, within 6 ft of sinks, laundry.

question: Per E3902.16, AFCI (arc-fault circuit-interrupter) protection is required for 120-volt, 15- and 20-ampere branch circuits supplying outlets in which dwelling areas?

options.B: Kitchens, family rooms, dining rooms, living rooms, bedrooms, hallways, closets, laundry areas, and similar rooms

explanation: E3902.16 requires AFCI protection on 120-volt, 15- and 20-ampere branch circuits supplying outlets and devices in nearly all habitable and similar spaces, including kitchens, family/dining/living rooms, bedrooms, parlors, libraries, dens, sunrooms, recreation rooms, closets, hallways, laundry areas, and similar rooms.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 E3902.16: AFCI on 120V 15/20 A circuits in nearly all dwelling living areas.

question: Per Table P3005.4.1 (drainage fixture units), a residential water closet (1.6 gpf) is assigned how many drainage fixture units (DFU)?
explanation: Under the drainage fixture unit values used for sizing DWV piping (Table P3004.1 / P3005), a private water closet at 1.6 gpf is assigned 3 drainage fixture units. DFU totals are used with Table P3005 to size the building drain and branch piping.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 DFU table (P3004.1): water closet 1.6 gpf = 3 DFU.

question: Per P3005.2.6, the maximum distance between cleanouts in horizontal drainage piping 4 inches or less in diameter must not exceed what?
explanation: P3005.2.6 requires cleanouts to be spaced not more than 100 feet apart in horizontal drainage lines that are 4 inches or less in nominal diameter (the limit is increased for larger pipe). This ensures the entire run can be cleared in the event of a stoppage.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 P3005.2.6: cleanout spacing max 100 ft for pipe 4 in or less.

question: Per P3005.2.2, a cleanout is required near the junction of the building drain and the building sewer. This cleanout is commonly required at what location?

options.B: At or near the connection between the building drain and building sewer, or an accessible alternative
options.C: Only inside the wall behind the water closet
options.D: Cleanouts are not required at the building drain

explanation: P3005.2.2 requires a cleanout near the junction of the building drain and the building sewer, either inside or outside the wall, accessible for rodding the sewer. An accessible two-way cleanout is often used to serve both the building drain and the sewer.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 P3005.2.2: cleanout at/near building drain-to-sewer junction.

question: Per P2804.6.1, the discharge pipe from a water heater temperature and pressure (T&P) relief valve must terminate not more than how far above the floor or waste receptor?
explanation: P2804.6.1 requires the relief valve discharge pipe to terminate not more than 6 inches and not less than two times the discharge pipe diameter above the floor, ground, or waste receptor flood-level rim. The discharge must be air gapped and not directly connected to drainage.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 P2804.6.1: T&P discharge terminates max 6 in above floor/receptor, min 2x pipe diameter, air gap.

question: Per P2804.6.1, the discharge piping from a relief valve must NOT do which of the following?

options.B: Have a reduced size smaller than the relief valve outlet, or contain a valve/trap, or thread its end
options.C: Be made of an approved material
options.D: Be installed with a downward slope

explanation: P2804.6.1 prohibits reducing the discharge pipe below the size of the relief valve outlet, installing a valve or check valve in the discharge, trapping the discharge line, and threading the terminal end (to prevent capping). The pipe must drain by gravity and discharge to an approved location.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 P2804.6.1: no size reduction, no valve/trap, no threaded terminal end; gravity drain.

question: Per P3007.3.3, a sump pit serving a sewage ejector/pump must have a minimum diameter and depth as specified, and the sump pump must be capable of handling solid spheres of at least what size for sewage?
explanation: Under P3007, sewage pumps and ejectors receiving the discharge of water closets must be capable of passing a 1-inch-diameter solid sphere. The sump pit must be constructed of approved materials and be airtight where it receives sewage, with proper venting.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 P3007.3: sewage pump for water closets must pass 1-in solid sphere.

question: Per P3007.1, a sump pit collecting clear-water or sewage discharge below the gravity drain must have a cover that is which of the following?

options.A: Open to the room for ventilation
options.B: Gas-tight and removable where it receives sewage

explanation: P3007 requires sump pits receiving sewage to have gastight, removable covers and to be vented in accordance with the venting provisions. A gas-tight cover prevents sewer gases from entering the dwelling.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 P3007: sewage sump cover gas-tight and removable, vented.

question: Per M1503.1 and M1503.4, where a range hood/domestic cooking exhaust is provided and discharges to the outdoors, the duct must be made of what?

options.B: Smooth interior, airtight metal duct equipped with a backdraft damper

explanation: M1503 requires range hood and domestic cooking exhaust ducts that discharge to the outdoors to be of smooth interior surface, airtight metal, equipped with a backdraft damper, and independent of all other exhaust systems. Single-wall ducts are required for grease-laden exhaust.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 M1503.4: range hood exhaust duct smooth airtight metal with backdraft damper, independent.

question: Per M1503.6, where a makeup air requirement is triggered, an exhaust hood system capable of exhausting in excess of how many cubic feet per minute (cfm) must be provided with makeup air?
explanation: M1503.6 requires that exhaust hood systems capable of exhausting in excess of 400 cfm be provided with makeup air at a rate approximately equal to the exhaust air rate, to avoid depressurization that can cause backdrafting of fuel-fired appliances.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 M1503.6: hoods over 400 cfm require makeup air.

question: Per M1602.2, return air must NOT be taken from which of the following locations?

options.B: A closet, bathroom, toilet room, kitchen, garage, mechanical room, boiler room, or furnace room

explanation: M1602.2 prohibits taking return air from closets, bathrooms, toilet rooms, kitchens, garages, mechanical rooms, boiler rooms, furnace rooms, and from spaces containing fuel-fired appliances (with exceptions), to prevent the circulation of contaminants and combustion products.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 M1602.2: prohibited return air from closets, baths, kitchens, garages, mechanical/boiler/furnace rooms.

question: Per G2427 (IFGC), a Category I gas appliance vent (such as a typical atmospheric draft furnace or water heater) operates under what condition?

options.B: Non-positive vent static pressure and non-condensing (does not condense flue gases)

explanation: Category I appliances operate with a non-positive vent static pressure and a vent gas temperature that avoids excessive condensate in the venting system (non-condensing). They are typically vented with Type B vent or a masonry/listed chimney sized per G2427.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 G2427 (IFGC venting): Category I = non-positive vent pressure, non-condensing.

question: Per M1601.4.4, metal ducts must be supported at intervals not exceeding what distance (for typical horizontal runs)?
explanation: M1601.4.4 requires metal ducts to be supported at intervals not exceeding 12 feet by approved hangers or supports. Other duct types (such as factory-made flexible or fibrous-glass ducts) have shorter support spacing per their listings and the code.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 M1601.4.4: metal ducts supported at intervals not exceeding 12 ft.

question: Per R1004.1, factory-built fireplaces must be listed and labeled and installed in accordance with what?

options.B: The conditions of the listing and the manufacturer's installation instructions

explanation: R1004.1 requires factory-built fireplaces to be listed and labeled and installed in accordance with the conditions of the listing and the manufacturer's instructions. They must also be tested to UL 127. Clearances to combustibles and hearth extensions follow the listing.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R1004.1: factory-built fireplaces listed/labeled, installed per listing and manufacturer instructions (UL 127).

question: Per R1005.1, factory-built chimneys serving factory-built fireplaces must be listed and labeled and tested to which standard?
explanation: R1005.1 requires factory-built chimneys to be listed and labeled and installed and terminated per the manufacturer's installation instructions. Factory-built chimneys for residential appliances are tested to UL 103 (and HT versions for higher temperatures).
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R1005.1: factory-built chimneys listed/labeled, tested to UL 103.

question: Per R1001.5, the firebox of a masonry fireplace must have a minimum depth (front to back) of how much for fireboxes 6 square feet or less in opening?
explanation: R1001.5 requires masonry fireplace fireboxes to have a minimum firebox depth of 20 inches. An exception allows a reduced depth for Rumford-style fireplaces meeting specific proportioning rules.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R1001.5: masonry firebox minimum depth 20 in (Rumford exception).

question: Per R1001.6, the firebox walls (lining) of a masonry fireplace lined with firebrick must be not less than what thickness?
explanation: R1001.6 requires firebox walls to be constructed of solid masonry not less than 8 inches thick, or lined with not less than 2 inches of firebrick where the total thickness (including lining) is at least 8 inches. The 2-inch firebrick lining provides heat resistance.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R1001.6: firebox 8 in solid masonry or 2 in firebrick lining within 8 in total.

question: Per Appendix V/AVII (residential pool barriers), the barrier surrounding a residential swimming pool must be at least what height measured on the side facing away from the pool?
explanation: The residential swimming pool barrier provisions require an enclosure (barrier) not less than 48 inches in height above grade measured on the side of the barrier facing away from the pool. Openings and gaps are also limited to prevent a young child from passing through.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 Appendix V residential pool barrier: minimum 48 in height on side away from pool. (Appendix designation/letter varies by adopting jurisdiction.)

question: Per the residential pool barrier provisions, the maximum vertical clearance between grade and the bottom of the barrier must not exceed what?
explanation: The pool barrier provisions limit the clearance between grade and the bottom of the barrier to a maximum of 2 inches when measured on the side facing away from the pool (4 inches where the barrier is mounted on top of an above-grade structure such as a deck). Openings must not allow passage of a 4-inch sphere.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 Appendix V pool barrier: bottom clearance 2 in (4 in on above-grade structure); openings reject 4-in sphere.

question: Per R309.1, the floor surface of a garage must be of approved noncombustible material, and the area of floor used for parking must be sloped to facilitate movement of liquids toward what?

options.B: A drain or toward the main vehicle entry doorway

explanation: R309.1 requires garage floors to be of approved noncombustible material, with the area used for parking sloped to facilitate the movement of liquids to a drain or toward the main vehicle entry doorway, preventing pooling of fuel or water.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R309.1: garage floor noncombustible, sloped to drain or main vehicle doorway.

question: Per R309.5, carports and garages must NOT open directly into which of the following rooms?

options.B: A room used for sleeping purposes

explanation: R309.5 prohibits openings from a private garage directly into a room used for sleeping purposes. Other doorways between a garage and dwelling must meet the separation and self-closing/solid-door requirements of R302.5.1.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R309.5: garage must not open directly into a sleeping room; door rules per R302.5.1.

question: Per Table R602.10.4, the minimum thickness of gypsum board used as a 'GB' braced wall panel applied to each face is what?
explanation: The gypsum board (GB) bracing method in Table R602.10.4 uses gypsum board not less than 1/2 inch thick, fastened to studs and applied to one or both faces depending on the required bracing length, with the contributing length doubled when applied to both faces.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 Table R602.10.4: GB method uses 1/2 in gypsum board; both-face application affects length.

question: Per R311.7.10.1, the minimum headroom for a spiral stairway, measured vertically from the tread nosings, must be not less than what?
explanation: R311.7.10.1 permits spiral stairways with a reduced minimum headroom of 6 feet 6 inches (versus 6 feet 8 inches for standard stairs), reflecting the compact geometry allowed specifically for spiral stairs.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R311.7.10.1: spiral stair headroom minimum 6 ft 6 in.

question: Per E3705 and the small-conductor overcurrent rules, a 15-ampere branch circuit using copper conductors must be wired with at least what conductor size?
explanation: The branch-circuit conductor sizing provisions require 14 AWG copper as the minimum for a 15-ampere branch circuit. The overcurrent device must not exceed the conductor's allowable ampacity for these small conductors.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 E3705.1: 15 A circuit = 14 AWG copper minimum.

question: Per E3905.12.2.1, a single yoke (strap) supporting one or more devices counts as how many conductor volume allowances, based on the largest conductor connected to the device?

options.B: Double volume allowance (two) based on the largest conductor connected to the device

explanation: E3905.12.2 requires a double volume allowance (two conductor volumes) for each yoke or strap containing one or more devices, based on the largest conductor connected to a device supported by that yoke. This accounts for the space occupied by the device.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 E3905.12.2: device yoke counts as double volume allowance (largest conductor at device).

question: Per Table R403.1(1), increasing the number of stories on a dwelling generally has what effect on the minimum required footing width (all else equal)?
explanation: Table R403.1(1) shows that as the number of supported stories increases, the minimum footing width increases (for a given soil bearing value) because the load transmitted to the soil is greater. Lower soil bearing values likewise require wider footings.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 Table R403.1(1): footing width increases with stories and with lower soil bearing value.

question: Per M1602.2, when return air is taken from one floor and supplied to another in a dwelling with multiple stories, the system must address what concern?

options.B: Air balance/transfer such that return air is not drawn from prohibited spaces and adequate makeup/transfer air is provided

explanation: M1602.2 and the duct system provisions require that return air be drawn only from permitted spaces and that adequate transfer/return paths exist. Return air must not be taken from prohibited rooms, and the system must maintain proper air balance between floors.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 M1602.2: return air only from permitted spaces; balance/transfer air required.

question: Per Table R301.5, guard in-fill components (such as balusters and panel fillers below the top rail) must be designed to resist a horizontally applied normal load of how many pounds on an area of 1 square foot?
explanation: Table R301.5 footnotes require guard in-fill components, balusters, and panel fillers to resist a horizontally applied normal load of 50 pounds on an area equal to 1 square foot. This is separate from the 200-pound concentrated load on the top of the guard.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 Table R301.5: in-fill components resist 50 lb on 1 sq ft (separate from 200 lb top load).

question: Under the IRC, what is the minimum CLEAR width that the required egress door of a dwelling unit must provide when the door is open 90 degrees?
explanation: The required egress door must provide a clear width of not less than 32 inches, measured between the face of the door and the stop with the door open 90 degrees. The door slab itself is typically wider (a 36-inch door is common) because the clear opening is reduced by the door thickness and stop.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R311.2 (ICC Digital Codes e up.codes): clear width >= 32 in, clear height >= 78 in, side-hinged.

question: What is the minimum CLEAR height of the required egress door opening, measured from the top of the threshold to the bottom of the stop?
explanation: The IRC requires the required egress door to provide a clear height of not less than 78 inches, measured from the top of the threshold to the bottom of the stop. A standard 6-foot 8-inch (80-inch) door slab yields this 78-inch clear height after the threshold and stop are accounted for.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R311.2: clear height >= 78 in.

question: When an attic access opening is provided in a ceiling, what is the minimum size of the required rough-framed opening?
explanation: Where an attic access is required, the rough-framed opening must be not less than 22 inches by 30 inches. When located in a ceiling, there must also be at least 30 inches of unobstructed headroom in the attic above the access, measured vertically.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R807.1: 22x30 rough opening, 30 in headroom, required where attic has clear height of 30 in over 30 sq ft.

question: An attic access opening is required when the attic area exceeds 30 square feet AND has a vertical clear height of at least how many inches?
explanation: An attic access opening is required for attics with combustible ceiling or roof construction that have a vertical height of 30 inches or greater over an area of not less than 30 square feet. If the attic is shorter than 30 inches OR smaller than 30 square feet, no access is required by R807.1.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R807.1: trigger = 30 in vertical height over 30 sq ft.

question: Per IRC Table R301.5, what is the minimum uniformly distributed live load for a habitable attic and attics served with a fixed stairway, as well as for sleeping rooms?
explanation: Table R301.5 assigns 30 psf to sleeping rooms and to habitable attics / attics served by fixed stairs. Non-sleeping living areas require 40 psf, while uninhabitable attics with limited storage are lower (10 or 20 psf).
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 Table R301.5: sleeping rooms = 30 psf; habitable attics & attics with fixed stairs = 30 psf; living areas = 40 psf.

question: Per IRC Table R301.5, what is the minimum uniformly distributed live load for living areas (non-sleeping habitable rooms such as a living room or kitchen)?
explanation: Living areas other than sleeping rooms must be designed for a 40 psf live load per Table R301.5. Sleeping rooms and habitable attics drop to 30 psf, while exterior balconies and decks are 40 psf as well.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 Table R301.5: living areas = 40 psf.

question: Per the IRC, notches in solid-lumber floor joists may not exceed what fraction of the depth of the member, and may not be located in the middle third of the span?
explanation: Notches in solid lumber joists, rafters and beams shall not exceed one-sixth of the depth, shall not be longer than one-third of the depth, and shall not be in the middle one-third of the span. Notches at the END of the member may go up to one-fourth the depth.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R502.8.1: notch <= 1/6 depth, <= 1/3 long, not in middle 1/3; end notch <= 1/4 depth.

question: Per the IRC, the diameter of a hole bored in a solid-lumber floor joist may not exceed what fraction of the depth of the member?
explanation: Bored holes may not exceed one-third the depth of the member and must be kept at least 2 inches from the top or bottom edge and at least 2 inches from any other hole or notch.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R502.8.1: bored hole <= 1/3 depth, 2 in clear from edges/other holes.

question: When boring a hole in a sawn-lumber rafter or ceiling joist, the hole must be kept at least how far from the top or bottom edge of the member (and from any other hole or notch)?
explanation: Holes bored in sawn-lumber rafters/joists must not be closer than 2 inches to the top or bottom of the member, nor closer than 2 inches to any other hole or to a notch in the same member. Diameter is still limited to one-third the depth.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R802.7.1: 2 in clearance to edges and other holes/notches.

question: When collar ties are used to resist rafter uplift near the ridge, what is the minimum size and maximum spacing required by the IRC?

options.A: Minimum 1x4 nominal, spaced not more than 48 inches on center
options.B: Minimum 2x4 nominal, spaced not more than 24 inches on center
options.C: Minimum 1x6 nominal, spaced not more than 32 inches on center
options.D: Minimum 2x6 nominal, spaced not more than 16 inches on center

explanation: Collar ties shall be not less than 1x4 nominal and spaced not more than 4 feet (48 inches) on center. They are installed in the upper third of the attic space to resist wind uplift at the ridge.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R802.4.6 (renumerado de R802.3.1 em edicoes antigas): 1x4 nominal min, 48 in o.c. max, upper third.

question: In which portion of the roof must collar ties be located when they are installed to connect opposing rafters?

options.A: In the lower third, near the wall plates
options.B: In the middle third of the rafter span
options.C: In the upper third of the attic space
options.D: Directly at the ridge board only

explanation: Collar ties (or ridge straps) connecting opposing rafters must be located in the upper third of the attic space. Ties placed lower in the assembly are functioning as rafter ties, which have separate requirements.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R802.4.6: collar ties in upper third of attic space.

question: In a wood-framed wall capped with a double top plate, end joints in the upper top plate must be offset from joints in the lower plate by at least how many inches?
explanation: End joints in the double top plate must be offset not less than 24 inches, and the plates must lap at corners and intersections with bearing partitions. Joints in the plate need not occur over a stud.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R602.3.2: double top plate end joints offset >= 24 in.

question: Per IRC Table R602.3(5), what is the maximum stud spacing for a 2x4 stud in an exterior bearing wall supporting a roof, ceiling, and one floor above, at the common 10-foot stud height?
explanation: For a 2x4 bearing-wall stud at the typical 10-foot maximum height carrying a roof plus one floor, the IRC stud table limits spacing to 16 inches on center. Lighter loading (roof and ceiling only) can allow 24 inches on center.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R602.3.1 / Table R602.3(5): 2x4 a 10 ft suportando teto+1 piso = 16 in o.c. max.

question: Absent frost considerations, the bottom of an exterior footing must be placed at least how far below the undisturbed ground surface?
explanation: The IRC requires the bottom of exterior footings to be at least 12 inches below the undisturbed ground surface. In areas subject to frost (such as Massachusetts), footings must extend below the local frost line, which governs when it is deeper than 12 inches.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R403.1.4: footing bottom >= 12 in below undisturbed grade, plus below frost line where applicable.

question: Per the IRC, backfill may not be placed against a foundation wall until the wall has been anchored to the floor above OR sufficiently braced. Below what unbalanced backfill height is that bracing requirement waived?
explanation: Per R404.1.7, backfill shall not be placed until the wall is anchored to the floor above or sufficiently braced. The exception waives this requirement for walls supporting 4 feet or less of unbalanced backfill. This is why crews typically frame the first floor before backfilling a full basement wall.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R404.1.7: bracing/anchorage required acima de 4 ft de unbalanced backfill.

question: In Seismic Design Categories D0, D1, or D2, masonry foundation walls supported only by the prescriptive provisions are limited to a maximum unbalanced backfill height of:
explanation: In SDC D0, D1, or D2, the unbalanced backfill height against prescriptive masonry foundation walls is limited to 4 feet. Most of Massachusetts is SDC B, where the standard backfill tables govern, but supervisors should know the seismic limit.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R404.1.4: masonry foundation walls em SDC D0/D1/D2 limitados a 4 ft unbalanced backfill.

question: Foundation walls that enclose below-grade interior space must be dampproofed (or waterproofed where severe water conditions exist) from the finished grade down to what point?

options.A: To the top of the footing only
options.B: To 6 inches below the top of the basement floor (or the top of the footing, whichever is higher)
options.C: To 12 inches above the basement floor
options.D: To the bottom of the footing

explanation: Both dampproofing (R406.1) and waterproofing (R406.2) must extend from the finished grade down to the higher of the top of the footing or 6 inches below the top of the basement floor. Waterproofing is the higher standard, required where a high water table or severe soil-water conditions exist.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R406.1 e R406.2: extensao ate o maior entre topo do footing ou 6 in abaixo do piso do basement.

question: Under IRC R506.2.3 (as adopted in the 2021 IRC), what is the minimum thickness/class of the vapor retarder required between a concrete slab-on-grade floor and the base course or subgrade?

options.C: 10-mil vapor retarder conforming to ASTM E1745 Class A
options.D: No vapor retarder is required for any slab

explanation: The 2021 IRC upgraded the under-slab vapor retarder requirement to a minimum 10-mil vapor retarder conforming to ASTM E1745 Class A, with joints lapped not less than 6 inches. Older codes accepted 6-mil poly.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R506.2.3: 10-mil ASTM E1745 Class A, lap 6 in. Mudanca chave vs edicoes anteriores (6-mil).

question: A masonry chimney must terminate at least how high above the highest point where it passes through the roof, and at least 2 feet higher than any portion of the building within 10 feet (the '3-2-10 rule')?
explanation: The 3-2-10 rule: a chimney must extend at least 3 feet above the highest point where it penetrates the roof, and at least 2 feet higher than any portion of the building within a 10-foot horizontal distance. Whichever condition produces the greater height governs.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R1003.9: 3 ft acima da penetracao no telhado, 2 ft acima de qualquer parte do edificio em 10 ft.

question: What is the minimum depth of the firebox of a standard (non-Rumford) masonry fireplace?
explanation: A standard masonry fireplace firebox must have a minimum depth of 20 inches. A Rumford fireplace is permitted with a shallower 12-inch depth provided it meets the additional R1001.6 throat and proportion requirements.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R1001.6: firebox depth >= 20 in (Rumford exception = 12 in).

question: For a masonry fireplace with an opening smaller than 6 square feet, the hearth extension must project at least how far in front of the fireplace opening?
explanation: For openings less than 6 square feet, the hearth extension must extend at least 16 inches in front of, and 8 inches beyond each side of, the opening. For openings of 6 square feet or larger, those minimums increase to 20 inches in front and 12 inches on each side.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R1001.10: opening < 6 sq ft = 16 in front / 8 in sides; >= 6 sq ft = 20 in / 12 in.

question: Asphalt shingles may be installed only on roof slopes of at least what minimum pitch?
explanation: Asphalt shingles require a minimum slope of 2:12. For slopes between 2:12 and 4:12, double underlayment is required. Below 2:12, asphalt shingles are not permitted.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R905.2.2: slope minimo 2:12 para asphalt shingles.

question: Absent a more restrictive manufacturer requirement, what is the minimum number of fasteners per strip (three-tab) asphalt shingle required by the IRC?
explanation: The IRC requires at least four fasteners per strip shingle (or two fasteners per individual shingle), unless the manufacturer's approved instructions require more (high-wind regions commonly require six).
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R905.2.6: min 4 fasteners por strip shingle (2 por individual), salvo manufacturer mais restritivo.

question: Per the IRC, a drip edge at the eaves and rake edges of a shingle roof must extend back onto the roof deck not less than:
explanation: A drip edge is required at eaves and rakes. It must extend back onto the roof deck not less than 2 inches and down below the sheathing not less than 1/4 inch, with adjacent segments lapped at least 2 inches.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R905.2.8.5: drip edge >= 2 in onto deck, >= 1/4 in below sheathing, 2 in laps.

question: A unit skylight installed on a roof with a slope of less than 3:12 must be mounted on a curb extending at least how high above the plane of the roof (unless the manufacturer specifies otherwise)?
explanation: Unit skylights on roofs with a pitch less than 3:12 must be mounted on a curb extending not less than 4 inches above the plane of the roof, unless the manufacturer's installation instructions allow otherwise.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R308.6.8: curb >= 4 in para skylight em slope < 3:12.

question: Habitable rooms must have an aggregate glazing (natural light) area of not less than what percentage of the floor area of the room?
explanation: Natural light: habitable rooms require glazing of at least 8 percent of the floor area. Natural ventilation requires openable area of at least 4 percent of the floor area. A common exam trap is swapping the 8 percent (light) and 4 percent (ventilation) values.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R303.1: light >= 8% floor area; ventilation openable >= 4% floor area.

question: Where natural ventilation is provided through openings to the outdoors, the openable area must be not less than what percentage of the floor area being ventilated?
explanation: Natural ventilation requires an openable area of at least 4 percent of the floor area being ventilated. The natural light requirement is the higher figure, 8 percent of floor area.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R303.1: ventilation openable >= 4% floor area.

question: For a ventilated under-floor (crawl) space WITHOUT an approved ground vapor retarder, the minimum net area of ventilation openings must be not less than:

options.A: 1 square foot per 100 square feet of crawl space ground area
options.B: 1 square foot per 150 square feet of crawl space ground area
options.C: 1 square foot per 300 square feet of crawl space ground area
options.D: 1 square foot per 1,500 square feet of crawl space ground area

explanation: Without a Class I vapor retarder on the ground, crawl space ventilation must be at least 1 square foot per 150 square feet of ground area. Where an approved ground vapor retarder is installed, the ratio may be reduced to 1 per 1,500.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R408.1: 1/150 sem vapor retarder; 1/1500 com vapor retarder aprovado (R408.2).

question: The minimum net free ventilating area for an enclosed attic or rafter space is what fraction of the area of the vented space (the standard, baseline ratio)?
explanation: The baseline attic ventilation requirement is 1/150 of the vented area. It can be reduced to 1/300 only when 40 to 50 percent of the venting is in the upper portion of the attic (and, in Climate Zones 6-8, a Class I or II vapor retarder is on the warm-in-winter side). Most of Massachusetts is Climate Zone 5.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R806.2: padrao 1/150; reducao a 1/300 com 40-50% high vents (e vapor retarder em CZ 6-8).

question: What is the minimum net clear opening AREA required for an emergency escape and rescue opening (other than a grade-floor/below-grade opening)?
explanation: Emergency escape and rescue openings must provide a net clear opening of at least 5.7 square feet. Grade-floor or below-grade openings may be reduced to 5.0 square feet. The opening also requires a minimum net clear height of 24 inches and width of 20 inches.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R310.2.1: net clear 5.7 sq ft (grade floor 5.0), height 24 in, width 20 in.

question: An emergency escape and rescue opening must have the bottom of the clear opening located no higher than how many inches above the finished floor?
explanation: The sill (bottom of the clear opening) of an emergency escape and rescue opening must be not more than 44 inches above the finished floor. The minimum net clear height is 24 inches and minimum width is 20 inches.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R310.2.2: sill height <= 44 in acima do piso acabado.

question: Under the IRC, what is the maximum riser height and minimum tread depth permitted for an interior stairway?

options.A: Maximum riser 8 inches, minimum tread 9 inches
options.B: Maximum riser 7-3/4 inches, minimum tread 10 inches
options.C: Maximum riser 7 inches, minimum tread 11 inches
options.D: Maximum riser 8-1/4 inches, minimum tread 9 inches

explanation: The IRC permits a maximum riser height of 7-3/4 inches and requires a minimum tread depth of 10 inches. Stairways must also be at least 36 inches wide and have a minimum headroom of 6 feet 8 inches. (The 7-inch riser / 11-inch tread figures belong to the IBC, a common distractor.)
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R311.7: riser max 7-3/4 in, tread min 10 in, width 36 in, headroom 6 ft 8 in.

question: Guards are required along open-sided walking surfaces, stairs, and landings located more than how many inches above the floor or grade below?
explanation: A guard is required where a walking surface is more than 30 inches above the floor or grade below (measured within 36 inches horizontally of the edge). Required residential guards must be at least 36 inches high.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R312.1.1/R312.1.2: guard exigido acima de 30 in; altura minima 36 in.

question: Per the IRC, the finished grade around a building must fall at least how much within the first 10 feet measured away from the foundation (where lot conditions permit)?
explanation: Lots must be graded to drain surface water away from the foundation; the grade must fall a minimum of 6 inches within the first 10 feet. Where physical barriers prevent that fall, swales or drains must be provided. Impervious surfaces within 10 feet must slope at least 2 percent away.
verifier_note: Confirmado contra IRC 2021 R401.3: 6 in de queda em 10 ft; impervious surfaces 2% min.

question: In the 2021 IRC, how is the maximum allowable cantilever (overhang) of a deck floor joist beyond its supporting beam determined?

options.A: It is always limited to exactly one-fourth of the joist backspan, regardless of joist size
options.B: It is taken from prescriptive Table R507.6 based on joist size, species, and span
options.C: It may equal the full backspan as long as the joists are doubled
options.D: Deck joists are never permitted to cantilever beyond the beam

explanation: The 2021 IRC replaced the old simple 'one-fourth of the backspan' rule with prescriptive maximum cantilever values listed in Table R507.6 by joist size, species, and span. Option A reflects the superseded rule, which is now a common exam distractor.
verifier_note: Confirmado: 2021 IRC moveu deck joists para R507.6 e substituiu a regra de 1/4 do backspan por Table R507.6. Distrator A = regra antiga (pre-2021).

question: Under OSHA excavation standards, at what trench or excavation depth must employees generally be protected from cave-ins by an adequate protective system (such as sloping, shoring, or shielding)?

options.A: Any excavation 3 feet or more in depth
options.B: Excavations 4 feet or more in depth
options.C: Excavations 5 feet or more in depth, unless made entirely in stable rock
options.D: Excavations 6 feet or more in depth

explanation: Each employee in an excavation must be protected from cave-ins by an adequate protective system, except when excavations are made entirely in stable rock OR are less than 5 feet deep and a competent person has examined the ground and found no indication of a potential cave-in. So protection is required at 5 feet and deeper (and sometimes shallower if the competent person finds a hazard).
verifier_note: Confirmed 5-foot trigger and stable-rock / competent-person exceptions in 1926.652(a)(1)(i)-(ii).

question: Under OSHA, excavated materials (spoil), equipment, and other materials must be kept at least how far back from the edge of an excavation?

options.A: At least 1 foot from the edge
options.B: At least 2 feet from the edge
options.C: At least 5 feet from the edge
options.D: Distance is left to the operator's discretion

explanation: Employees must be protected from excavated or other materials or equipment that could pose a hazard by falling or rolling into excavations. Protection is provided by keeping such materials at least 2 feet from the edge of the excavation, by using retaining devices, or by a combination of both.
verifier_note: Confirmed 2-foot spoil-setback requirement in 1926.651(j)(2).

question: In a trench excavation 4 feet or more in depth, a stairway, ladder, ramp, or other safe means of egress must be located so that an employee never has to travel laterally more than:
explanation: A stairway, ladder, ramp or other safe means of egress must be located in trench excavations that are 4 feet or more in depth so as to require no more than 25 feet of lateral travel for employees.
verifier_note: Confirmed 4-foot depth trigger and 25-foot max lateral travel to egress in 1926.651(c)(2).

question: Under OSHA's soil classification system in Appendix A to Subpart P, which soil type is the MOST stable?
explanation: Stable rock is the most stable classification (it can be excavated with vertical sides and remain intact). Among soils, Type A is most stable, Type B is intermediate, and Type C is the least stable. The standard classifies materials as Stable Rock, Type A, Type B, and Type C.
verifier_note: Confirmed classification order (Stable Rock most stable, then A, B, C) in Subpart P Appendix A. Question asks for the single most stable category overall, which is stable rock.

question: Under OSHA scaffold standards, each scaffold and scaffold component must be capable of supporting, without failure, its own weight and at least how many times the maximum intended load applied or transmitted to it?
explanation: Each scaffold and scaffold component must support, without failure, its own weight and at least 4 times the maximum intended load applied or transmitted to it. (Suspension ropes and connecting hardware have a separate 6-to-1 safety factor.)
verifier_note: Confirmed 4x capacity factor for scaffolds/components in 1926.451(a)(1); 6x for suspension ropes in 1926.451(a)(4).

question: Each employee on a scaffold more than how many feet above a lower level must be protected from falling by guardrails or a personal fall arrest system?
explanation: Each employee on a scaffold more than 10 feet above a lower level must be protected from falling to that lower level by guardrail systems and/or personal fall arrest systems (with specific rules varying by scaffold type). Note: the general construction fall-protection trigger for unprotected edges is 6 feet, but the scaffold-specific threshold is 10 feet.
verifier_note: Confirmed scaffold 10-foot fall-protection trigger in 1926.451(g)(1). Distractor B (6 ft) is intentionally the general Subpart M edge trigger.

question: For scaffolds manufactured or first placed in service after January 1, 2000, the top edge height of toprails (or equivalent guardrail members) must be between:

options.A: 30 inches and 36 inches above the platform surface
options.B: 36 inches and 45 inches above the platform surface
options.C: 38 inches and 45 inches above the platform surface
options.D: 42 inches and 48 inches above the platform surface

explanation: For scaffolds manufactured and placed in service after January 1, 2000, the top edge height of toprails or equivalent guardrail members must be between 38 inches and 45 inches above the platform surface. (Scaffolds placed in service before that date allowed 36 to 45 inches.)
verifier_note: Confirmed 38-45 in toprail height for post-Jan-1-2000 scaffolds in 1926.451(g)(4)(ii). Distractor B (36-45 in) is the pre-2000 figure.

question: Under OSHA scaffold rules, when scaffold platforms are more than how many feet above or below a point of access, must a safe means of access (ladder, stair tower, ramp, etc.) be provided?
explanation: When scaffold platforms are more than 2 feet above or below a point of access, a safe means of access such as a portable ladder, hook-on ladder, attachable ladder, stair tower, stairway-type ladder, ramp, or direct access from another scaffold or structure must be provided. Cross braces may NOT be used as a means of access.
verifier_note: Confirmed 2-foot access trigger and prohibition on using cross braces for access in 1926.451(e)(1).

question: Under OSHA construction electrical standards, controls that are deactivated during the course of work on energized or de-energized equipment or circuits must be:

options.A: Left as found so other crews can re-energize quickly
options.B: Tagged with a label, and the tags must remain in place until the work is completed

explanation: Controls that are to be deactivated during the course of work on energized or de-energized equipment or circuits must be tagged. Tags must be placed to plainly identify the equipment or circuits being worked on, and must remain in place until the work is completed.
verifier_note: Confirmed control-tagging and tag-remains-until-complete requirements in 1926.417(a) and (b).

question: On construction sites, which of the following is an acceptable method for protecting employees from ground-fault hazards on 120-volt, single-phase, 15- and 20-ampere receptacle outlets not part of the permanent wiring?

options.A: Using only double-insulated tools with no further protection
options.B: Ground-fault circuit interrupters (GFCIs) OR an assured equipment grounding conductor program
options.C: A surge protector strip plugged into the outlet
options.D: Posting a sign warning of shock hazard

explanation: OSHA requires either ground-fault circuit interrupters (GFCIs) for all 120V, single-phase, 15- and 20-amp receptacle outlets not part of the permanent wiring, OR the employer must establish and implement an assured equipment grounding conductor program covering all cord sets, receptacles not part of the permanent wiring, and equipment connected by cord and plug.
verifier_note: Confirmed the GFCI vs. assured-grounding-program options in 1926.404(b)(1)(i)-(iii).

question: Under OSHA construction PPE rules, employees working in areas where there is a possible danger of head injury from impact, falling or flying objects, or electrical shock and burns must be protected by:

options.D: Nothing, if they are experienced workers

explanation: Employees working in areas where there is a possible danger of head injury from impact, or from falling or flying objects, or from electrical shock and burns, must be protected by protective helmets (hard hats) meeting the applicable ANSI/consensus standard.
verifier_note: Confirmed head-protection requirement in 1926.100(a).

question: Under OSHA, eye and face protective equipment must be provided when:

options.A: Only when the employee specifically requests it
options.B: Machines or operations present potential eye or face injury from physical, chemical, or radiation agents
options.D: Only after an injury has already occurred on the job

explanation: Employees must be provided with eye and face protection equipment when machines or operations present potential eye or face injury from physical, chemical, or radiation agents. The equipment must meet the applicable ANSI standard for occupational and educational eye and face protection.
verifier_note: Confirmed eye/face protection trigger in 1926.102(a)(1).

question: Under OSHA construction noise standards, feasible administrative or engineering controls must be used to reduce noise exposure when sound levels exceed which 8-hour permissible exposure level?
explanation: Protection against the effects of noise exposure must be provided when sound levels exceed a 90 dBA slow-response level on an 8-hour time-weighted basis (the construction PEL). When engineering or administrative controls fail to reduce levels within the table limits, personal hearing protective equipment must be provided and used.
verifier_note: Confirmed 90 dBA 8-hr PEL and the controls requirement in 1926.52(b) and Table D-2. Distractor B (85 dBA) is the general-industry action level, not the construction PEL.

question: Under the OSHA Hazard Communication standard as applied to construction, what must an employer ensure is readily accessible to employees for each hazardous chemical they may be exposed to?

options.A: A purchase receipt for the chemical
options.B: The chemical manufacturer's phone number only
options.C: A Safety Data Sheet (SDS) and a properly labeled container
options.D: A verbal warning from the supervisor only

explanation: Under the Hazard Communication standard (which applies to construction), employers must maintain Safety Data Sheets (SDS) for each hazardous chemical and ensure they are readily accessible to employees during each work shift, ensure containers are labeled, and provide employee information and training.
verifier_note: 1926.59 adopts 1910.1200 by reference for construction. SDS-accessibility requirement confirmed in 1910.1200(g)(8); labeling in (f); access requirement in (g)(8). The term MSDS was replaced by SDS under the 2012 GHS-aligned update.

question: Under OSHA's cranes and derricks in construction standard, when a signal person is required and visual contact cannot be maintained, which of the following is true?

options.A: Signaling may stop entirely until visibility returns
options.B: The operator may continue the lift without any signaling method
options.C: Hand signals are still the only permitted method
options.D: An alternative signal method (such as radio/voice or audible signals) must be used and understood by both operator and signal person

explanation: Hand signals, voice signals, and audible signals are all permissible methods under the standard. When hand signals are used they must follow the standard system; when other methods (e.g., radio) are used they must be agreed upon and understood. Only ONE method may be used at a time, and the operator and signal person must agree on the method. A signal person is required when, among other conditions, the operator's view is obstructed.
verifier_note: Confirmed permissible signal methods (hand/voice/audible/new) and the requirement to agree on a method in 1926.1419; signal-person-required conditions in 1926.1428.

question: Under OSHA construction stairway rules, a stairrail system must be installed along each unprotected side or edge of stairways having how many or more risers, or rising more than 30 inches, whichever is less?
explanation: Stairways having four or more risers or rising more than 30 inches, whichever is less, must be equipped with at least one handrail and one stairrail system along each unprotected side or edge.
verifier_note: Confirmed '4 or more risers or rising more than 30 inches, whichever is less' in 1926.1052(c)(1).

question: Under OSHA, riser height and tread depth on temporary construction stairways must be uniform within each flight of stairs, with variations not to exceed:
explanation: Stairways must be installed so that riser height and tread depth are uniform within each flight, including any foundation structure used as one or more treads of the stairs. Variations in riser height or tread depth must not exceed 1/4 inch in any stairway system.
verifier_note: Confirmed 1/4-inch max variation in riser/tread uniformity in 1926.1052(a)(3).

subtopic: Concrete and Masonry - Limited Access Zone
question: Under OSHA's masonry construction rules, when a masonry wall is being constructed, a limited access zone must be established prior to the start of construction. How wide must this zone be?

options.A: Equal to the height of the wall to be constructed plus 4 feet
options.B: A fixed 6 feet regardless of wall height
options.C: Equal to twice the wall thickness
options.D: Equal to the wall height divided by 2

explanation: A limited access zone must be established whenever a masonry wall is being constructed. It must be equal to the height of the wall to be constructed plus 4 feet, run the entire length of the wall, and be on the side of the wall that will be unscaffolded. It must be restricted to entry only by employees actively engaged in constructing the wall.
verifier_note: Confirmed 'wall height plus 4 feet' limited access zone in 1926.706(a)(1)-(2).

question: Under OSHA concrete and masonry construction rules, all protruding reinforcing steel (rebar), onto and into which employees could fall, must be:

options.B: Guarded to eliminate the hazard of impalement
options.D: Cut flush with the concrete only after curing

explanation: All protruding reinforcing steel, onto and into which employees could fall, must be guarded to eliminate the hazard of impalement. This is commonly achieved with rebar caps or troughs rated for the application.
verifier_note: Confirmed rebar-impalement guarding requirement in 1926.701(b).

question: Under OSHA demolition rules, prior to permitting employees to start demolition operations, what must be conducted by a competent person?

options.A: A cost estimate of the demolition
options.B: An engineering survey of the structure, including condition of the structure and possibility of unplanned collapse
options.C: A marketing survey of the neighborhood
options.D: A survey is only required for buildings over 10 stories

explanation: Prior to permitting employees to start demolition operations, an engineering survey must be made by a competent person of the structure to determine the condition of the framing, floors, and walls, and the possibility of unplanned collapse of any portion of the structure. The employer must also have in writing evidence that such a survey was performed.
verifier_note: Confirmed competent-person engineering survey requirement before demolition in 1926.850(a).

question: Under OSHA welding and cutting rules, when must a fire watch be required during welding or cutting operations?

options.B: Where appreciable combustible material, in building construction or contents, is closer than 35 feet to the point of operation (or in other specified high-hazard conditions)
options.C: Only for jobs lasting more than 8 hours
options.D: Never; sprinklers always substitute for a fire watch

explanation: Fire watchers are required whenever welding or cutting is performed in locations where other than a minor fire might develop, including where appreciable combustible material (in building construction or contents) is closer than 35 feet to the point of operation, or where combustibles are more than 35 feet away but are easily ignited by sparks. A fire watch must be maintained for at least a half hour after completion of operations to detect and extinguish smoldering fires.
verifier_note: Confirmed fire-watch conditions tied to 35-foot combustible distance in 1926.352(e); the half-hour post-watch is from the referenced NFPA-based provisions and general fire-prevention rule 1926.352(e).

question: Under current OSHA reporting requirements, within what time frame must an employer report a work-related fatality to OSHA?
explanation: Employers must report work-related fatalities to OSHA within 8 hours of finding out about them. By contrast, work-related in-patient hospitalizations, amputations, or losses of an eye must be reported within 24 hours.
verifier_note: Confirmed 8-hour fatality reporting and 24-hour hospitalization/amputation/eye-loss reporting in 1904.39(a)(1)-(2). Recordkeeping is Part 1904, which applies across construction employers.

question: Within what time frame must an employer report a work-related in-patient hospitalization, amputation, or loss of an eye to OSHA?
explanation: Employers must report any work-related in-patient hospitalization of one or more employees, amputation, or loss of an eye to OSHA within 24 hours. Fatalities, by contrast, must be reported within 8 hours.
verifier_note: Confirmed 24-hour reporting window for hospitalization/amputation/eye-loss in 1904.39(a)(2). Distractor A (8 hr) is intentionally the fatality window.

question: Under OSHA excavation standards, daily inspections of excavations, adjacent areas, and protective systems must be made by:

options.B: A competent person, before the start of work and as needed throughout the shift

explanation: Daily inspections of excavations, the adjacent areas, and protective systems must be made by a competent person for evidence of a situation that could result in possible cave-ins, failure of protective systems, hazardous atmospheres, or other hazardous conditions. Inspections must be conducted prior to the start of work and as needed throughout the shift, and after every rainstorm or other hazard-increasing occurrence.
verifier_note: Confirmed competent-person daily inspection requirement and timing in 1926.651(k)(1).

question: Under OSHA Subpart M, each employee on a walking/working surface with an unprotected side or edge that is how many feet or more above a lower level must be protected from falling?
explanation: Under Subpart M, each employee on a walking/working surface with an unprotected side or edge 6 feet or more above a lower level must be protected by guardrail systems, safety net systems, or personal fall arrest systems. (4 feet is the general-industry trigger; scaffolds use 10 feet; steel erection has its own rules.)
verifier_note: Confirmed 6-foot general construction fall-protection trigger in 1926.501(b)(1). Distractor A (4 ft) is general industry; distractor C (10 ft) is scaffolds.

question: Under OSHA scaffold rules, the erection, moving, dismantling, or alteration of scaffolds must be done under the supervision and direction of:

options.B: A competent person qualified in scaffold erection

explanation: The erection, moving, dismantling, or alteration of scaffolds must be done under the supervision and direction of a competent person qualified in scaffold erection, moving, dismantling, or alteration. Such activities must be performed only by experienced and trained employees selected for such work by the competent person.
verifier_note: Confirmed competent-person supervision of scaffold erection/dismantling in 1926.451(f)(7).

subtopic: Accessible Parking - Count by Lot Size
question: Under 521 CMR, in a parking lot with a total of 100 spaces, what is the minimum number of accessible (handicap) parking spaces required?
explanation: Per the 521 CMR 23.00 accessible parking table, a lot of 76 to 100 total spaces requires 4 accessible spaces. (1-25 requires 1; 26-50 requires 2; 51-75 requires 3; 76-100 requires 4; the count then increases on a sliding scale for larger lots.)
verifier_note: Confirmed 521 CMR 23.2 parking table: 76-100 spaces = 4 accessible. Same tiering matches the federal ADAAG table, which 521 CMR mirrors at the low end.

question: Under 521 CMR, a parking lot with 1 to 25 total spaces must provide at least how many accessible parking spaces?

options.D: 1 accessible space for every 10 total spaces

explanation: Per the 521 CMR 23.00 accessible parking table, even the smallest covered lots (1 to 25 total spaces) must provide a minimum of 1 accessible parking space. The required count rises with lot size (26-50 requires 2, 51-75 requires 3, and so on).
verifier_note: Confirmed 521 CMR 23.2 minimum of 1 accessible space for 1-25-space lots.

question: Under 521 CMR accessible parking requirements, which of the following best describes the van-accessible space requirement?

options.B: At least one in every eight accessible spaces (but always at least one) must be van-accessible and identified by a sign reading 'Van Accessible'
options.C: All accessible spaces must be van-accessible
options.D: Van-accessible spaces are only required at hospitals

explanation: One in every eight accessible spaces, but not fewer than one, must be van-accessible. Van-accessible spaces have a wider access aisle (or wider space) to accommodate a van with a side-mounted lift or ramp and must be identified by an additional sign stating 'Van Accessible' mounted below the symbol of access.
verifier_note: Confirmed 1-in-8 van-accessible ratio (min 1) and the 'Van Accessible' sign requirement in 521 CMR 23, consistent with the ADAAG ratio that 521 CMR adopts.

question: Under 521 CMR, the minimum clear opening width of an accessible doorway, measured with the door open 90 degrees between the face of the door and the stop, must be at least:
explanation: Per 521 CMR 26.00, doorways must have a minimum clear opening of 32 inches, measured with the door open 90 degrees between the face of the door and the door stop. Openings more than 24 inches deep require a 36-inch minimum clear opening.
verifier_note: Confirmed 32-inch min clear door opening in 521 CMR 26, matching the federal 32-inch standard the AAB adopts.

question: Under 521 CMR, for a front (forward) approach to a swinging door on the PULL side, the minimum maneuvering clearance includes how much space beyond the latch side of the door?
explanation: For a front approach on the pull side of a swinging door, 521 CMR requires 18 inches of clearance beyond the latch side of the door (and 60 inches of clear depth perpendicular to the doorway) so a wheelchair user can position to open the door. This mirrors the federal maneuvering-clearance requirement.
