verifier_note: Confirmed 18-inch latch-side clearance for front pull-side approach in 521 CMR 26, consistent with ADAAG door maneuvering clearance tables the AAB adopts.

question: Under 521 CMR, what is the maximum rise for any single ramp run before a level landing is required?
explanation: Per 521 CMR 24.00, the maximum rise for any single ramp run is 30 inches. A level landing is required at the top and bottom of each run, and landings must be at least 60 inches long in the direction of travel.
verifier_note: Confirmed 30-inch max ramp rise and 60-inch landing length in 521 CMR 24, consistent with onda-1 aab-006 (1:12 slope) and ADAAG.

question: Under 521 CMR, a level landing at the top and bottom of an accessible ramp must have a minimum length, in the direction of travel, of:
explanation: Per 521 CMR 24.00, ramps must have level landings at the bottom and top of each run, and each landing must be at least 60 inches long in the direction of travel. The landing must be at least as wide as the widest ramp run leading to it.
verifier_note: Confirmed 60-inch min landing length in 521 CMR 24, matching the federal ramp-landing standard adopted by the AAB.

question: Under 521 CMR, ramps with a rise greater than how many inches must have handrails on both sides?
explanation: Per 521 CMR 24.00, ramps with a rise greater than 6 inches or a horizontal projection greater than 72 inches must have handrails on both sides. (Handrails are not required on curb ramps or adjacent to seating in assembly areas.)
verifier_note: Confirmed handrails required on ramps with rise > 6 in or run > 72 in in 521 CMR 24, consistent with ADAAG.

question: Under 521 CMR, the minimum clear width of an accessible route (for a single wheelchair) is generally:

options.B: 32 inches at a point and 36 inches continuously

explanation: Per 521 CMR 20.00, the minimum clear width of an accessible route is 36 inches continuously, with a reduction to 32 inches allowed at a point (such as at a doorway) for a maximum depth of 24 inches. Where an accessible route is less than 60 inches wide, passing spaces at least 60 by 60 inches must be provided at reasonable intervals.
verifier_note: Confirmed 36-inch continuous / 32-inch-at-a-point accessible route width and 60x60 passing spaces in 521 CMR 20, mirroring ADAAG.

question: Under 521 CMR, in an accessible water closet (toilet) compartment, grab bars must be mounted with their top gripping surface at what height above the finished floor?
explanation: Per 521 CMR 30.00, grab bars at accessible water closets must be mounted with the top of the gripping surface 33 to 36 inches above the finished floor. The diameter of the gripping surface must be 1-1/4 to 1-1/2 inches, with 1-1/2 inches clearance between the bar and the wall.
verifier_note: Confirmed 33-36 inch grab-bar mounting height and 1.5-inch wall clearance in 521 CMR 30, consistent with the ADAAG grab-bar standard adopted by the AAB.

subtopic: Accessible Toilet Rooms - Water Closet Centerline
question: Under 521 CMR, in an accessible toilet compartment, the centerline of the water closet (toilet) must be located what distance from the adjacent side wall?

options.B: 16 to 18 inches from the side wall

explanation: Per 521 CMR 30.00, the centerline of an accessible water closet must be 16 to 18 inches from the adjacent side wall or partition (18 inches is the standard target). This positions the toilet so a wheelchair user can transfer using the side grab bar.
verifier_note: Confirmed 16-18 inch water-closet centerline from side wall in 521 CMR 30, matching ADAAG (which specifies 16-18 in, 18 in for standard stalls).

question: Under 521 CMR, accessible facilities and elements required to be identified must display which symbol?

options.B: The International Symbol of Access (ISA), a white wheelchair figure on a blue background
options.D: No symbol is required if a sign reads 'Accessible'

explanation: Per 521 CMR 41.00, accessible parking spaces, entrances, toilet rooms, and other required accessible elements must be identified by the International Symbol of Access (ISA) -- the standardized wheelchair figure, displayed in white on a blue background for parking signage.
verifier_note: Confirmed ISA signage requirement in 521 CMR 41; white-on-blue color for accessible parking signs is the Massachusetts/MUTCD convention.

question: Under 521 CMR, permanent room and space identification signs (such as restroom signs with raised characters and Braille) must be mounted so the centerline of the sign is at what height above the finished floor?
explanation: Per 521 CMR 41.00, signs designating permanent rooms and spaces must be mounted with the centerline of the sign 60 inches above the finished floor, and mounted on the wall adjacent to the latch side of the door so a person can approach within 3 inches without encountering protruding objects or the door swing.
verifier_note: Confirmed 60-inch centerline mounting and latch-side placement for permanent room ID signs in 521 CMR 41, consistent with the ADAAG signage provisions adopted by the AAB.

question: Under 521 CMR, in a newly constructed multi-story public building, when is at least one accessible elevator (or other approved vertical access) generally required?

options.B: Never; ramps always substitute for elevators
options.C: When the building has more than one story and an accessible route to upper floors is required, an accessible elevator must be provided (subject to specific exceptions)

explanation: Per 521 CMR 28.00, accessible elevators are required to connect each level in multi-story public buildings where an accessible route is required to upper or lower floors. 521 CMR is generally MORE stringent than the federal ADA, which exempts certain small two-story or low-occupancy buildings; in Massachusetts elevators are required in most multi-story public buildings, subject to the specific exceptions and thresholds in 521 CMR.
verifier_note: Confirmed general elevator/vertical-access requirement for multi-story public buildings in 521 CMR 28; AAB is stricter than ADA on the small-building elevator exemption. Question is scoped to the general rule and notes 'subject to specific exceptions' to avoid overstating a single numeric threshold.

question: Under 521 CMR, the maximum running slope of a curb cut (curb ramp) is:
explanation: Per 521 CMR 21.00 / 24.00, curb cuts must have a maximum running slope of 1:12, the same as accessible ramps. The maximum cross slope is 1:50 (2%), and flared sides, where provided, must not exceed a slope of 1:10.
verifier_note: Confirmed 1:12 curb-ramp running slope and 1:10 flared-side slope in 521 CMR, consistent with onda-1 aab-006 ramp slope and ADAAG curb-ramp provisions.

question: Under 521 CMR, accessible parking spaces must be identified by a sign showing the symbol of access. To remain visible (not obscured by a parked vehicle), the bottom of the sign should be mounted approximately how high?

options.C: A minimum of about 60 inches above grade (so it is visible above a parked vehicle)

explanation: Per 521 CMR 23.00, accessible parking spaces must be identified by a vertical sign bearing the International Symbol of Access mounted high enough to be visible while a vehicle is parked in the space -- generally with the sign mounted so its bottom edge is about 60 inches or higher above grade. Pavement markings alone are not sufficient.
verifier_note: Confirmed vertical-sign requirement (not pavement-only) and visible-above-vehicle mounting in 521 CMR 23; the ~60-inch minimum-to-bottom is the standard AAB/ADA convention to keep the symbol visible above a parked car.

question: Under OSHA construction standards, which of the following BEST describes a "competent person"?

options.A: An employee who has completed a 10-hour OSHA outreach course
options.B: One who is capable of identifying existing and predictable hazards and is authorized to take prompt corrective measures to eliminate them
options.C: A licensed professional engineer retained by the general contractor
options.D: Any worker designated by the owner regardless of training or authority

explanation: OSHA defines a competent person as one who is capable of identifying existing and predictable hazards in the surroundings or working conditions which are hazardous or dangerous to employees, AND who has authorization to take prompt corrective measures to eliminate them. Both hazard recognition and the authority to act are required.
verifier_note: Confirmed against OSHA 1926.32(f) definitions page. Two-part test (identify hazards + authority to correct) is verbatim from the standard.

question: An anchorage used for the attachment of personal fall arrest equipment must be capable of supporting at least how many pounds per employee attached (when not designed as part of a complete system with a safety factor of two)?
explanation: Anchorages for personal fall arrest equipment must be independent of any anchorage used to support or suspend platforms and must support at least 5,000 pounds per employee attached, unless the system is designed and used with a safety factor of at least two times the maximum arresting force. Note: 3,000 pounds is the figure for POSITIONING devices, not fall arrest anchorages.
verifier_note: Confirmed 5,000 lb per employee for fall ARREST anchorages in 1926.502(d)(15). Distractor B (3,000 lb) is intentionally the positioning-device figure from 1926.502(e)(2).

question: A positioning device system (such as a body belt used to hold a worker on a wall to keep both hands free) must be secured to an anchorage capable of supporting at least:

options.A: Twice the potential impact load of a fall or 3,000 pounds, whichever is greater

explanation: Positioning device systems must be secured to an anchorage capable of supporting at least twice the potential impact load of an employee's fall, or 3,000 pounds, whichever is greater. This is distinct from the 5,000-pound requirement for personal fall ARREST anchorages.
verifier_note: Confirmed 'twice the potential impact load or 3,000 lb, whichever is greater' in 1926.502(e)(2). Distractor B is the fall-arrest figure; distractor C is the guardrail top-rail force.

question: Under the OSHA lead in construction standard, the permissible exposure limit (PEL) for airborne lead, averaged over an 8-hour time-weighted average, is:

options.A: 30 micrograms per cubic meter of air
options.B: 50 micrograms per cubic meter of air
options.C: 100 micrograms per cubic meter of air
options.D: 400 micrograms per cubic meter of air

explanation: The employer must ensure no employee is exposed to lead at concentrations greater than 50 micrograms per cubic meter of air (50 ug/m3) averaged over an 8-hour period. The 30 ug/m3 figure is the ACTION LEVEL, not the PEL.
verifier_note: Confirmed PEL 50 ug/m3 8-hr TWA in 1926.62(c)(1). Distractor A (30 ug/m3) is intentionally the action level from 1926.62(b).

question: On a construction site, a fire extinguisher rated not less than 2A must be provided for each ____ of protected building area, or major fraction thereof.
explanation: OSHA requires one fire extinguisher rated not less than 2A for each 3,000 square feet of protected building area, or major fraction thereof. Travel distance to the nearest extinguisher cannot exceed 100 feet.
verifier_note: Confirmed 2A per 3,000 sq ft and 100 ft max travel distance in 1926.150(c)(1)(i) and (iv).

question: When a portable ladder is used for access to an upper landing surface, the side rails must extend at least how far above the landing surface (or, if not possible, be secured with a grasping device provided)?
explanation: When portable ladders are used for access to an upper landing surface, the ladder side rails must extend at least 3 feet above the landing. If the ladder's length makes this impossible, the ladder must be secured at the top to a rigid support and a grasping device (grab rail) provided.
verifier_note: Confirmed 3-foot side-rail extension in 1926.1053(b)(1). Note: this is distinct from the 4:1 (1 ft out per 4 ft of height) setup ratio in 1926.1053(b)(5)(i).

question: A non-self-supporting (leaning) portable ladder must be positioned so that the horizontal distance from the base to the support is approximately what fraction of the working length of the ladder?
explanation: Non-self-supporting ladders must be used at an angle where the horizontal distance from the top support to the foot of the ladder is approximately one-quarter of the working length of the ladder (the 4:1 rule, roughly a 75-degree angle).
verifier_note: Confirmed 1:4 ratio (horizontal distance = 1/4 of working length) in 1926.1053(b)(5)(i).

question: On a construction operation over or near water where the danger of drowning exists, ring buoys with at least 90 feet of line must be readily available, spaced so that the distance between any two ring buoys does not exceed:
explanation: Ring buoys with at least 90 feet of line must be provided and readily available for emergency rescue, and the distance between ring buoys cannot exceed 200 feet. At least one lifesaving skiff must also be immediately available, and U.S. Coast Guard-approved life jackets or buoyant work vests must be provided.
verifier_note: Confirmed 90 ft of line and 200 ft max spacing in 1926.106(c); skiff requirement in 1926.106(d).

question: According to OSHA, a DANGER sign must use which color scheme?

options.A: Yellow predominating, with black panel and borders
options.B: Red as the predominating color on the upper panel, black outline borders, and a white lower panel for wording
options.C: Green upper panel with white letters on a white background

explanation: Danger signs must have red as the predominating color for the upper panel, black outlines on the borders, and a white lower panel for additional sign wording. They are used only where an immediate hazard exists. (Yellow predominating = CAUTION sign; green upper panel = safety instruction sign.)
verifier_note: Confirmed danger sign colors (red/black/white) in 1926.200(b). Distractor A is the caution-sign scheme; distractor C is the safety-instruction-sign scheme.

question: Under OSHA housekeeping requirements for construction, how must combustible scrap and debris be handled during the course of construction?

options.A: Left in place until the project is complete, then removed all at once
options.B: Removed at regular intervals during the course of construction
options.C: Burned on site at the end of each work day
options.D: Removed only when it blocks an exit

explanation: OSHA requires that combustible scrap and debris be removed at regular intervals during the course of construction, and that safe means be provided to facilitate removal. Additionally, form and scrap lumber with protruding nails and all other debris must be kept cleared from work areas, passageways, and stairs.
verifier_note: Confirmed 'removed at regular intervals' for combustible debris in 1926.25(b); cleared-debris requirement in 1926.25(a).

question: Under 521 CMR (Massachusetts Architectural Access Board), if alteration, reconstruction, or remodeling work on an existing public building equals or exceeds 30% of the full and fair cash value of the building, what is required?

options.A: Only the work being performed must comply
options.B: Only an accessible entrance must be added
options.C: The entire building is required to comply with 521 CMR
options.D: No accessibility upgrades are required for existing buildings

explanation: Under 521 CMR 3.3, when the work performed (including exempted work) amounts to 30% or more of the full and fair cash value of the building, the ENTIRE building must comply with 521 CMR, not just the altered area.
verifier_note: Confirmed 30% full-and-fair-cash-value trigger for full-building compliance in 521 CMR 3.3.

question: Under 521 CMR 3.3, when work on an existing building is LESS than 30% of full and fair cash value but the cost of the work is $100,000 or more, what must be provided in addition to making the work comply?

options.A: Nothing additional; only the work performed must comply
options.B: An accessible public entrance and, if provided, an accessible toilet room, telephone, and drinking fountain
options.C: A full elevator serving every floor

explanation: Under 521 CMR 3.3, if work is under 30% of value but costs $100,000 or more, the work must comply AND an accessible public entrance must be provided, plus an accessible toilet room, telephone, and drinking fountain if those facilities exist. (If the work costs under $100,000, only the work itself must comply.)
verifier_note: Confirmed the $100,000 threshold adding entrance/toilet/telephone/drinking-fountain requirement in 521 CMR 3.3, distinct from the under-$100,000 (work-only) and 30% (whole-building) tiers.

question: Under 521 CMR, the minimum clear floor or ground space required to accommodate a single, stationary wheelchair and occupant is:
explanation: 521 CMR specifies a minimum clear floor or ground space of 30 inches by 48 inches to accommodate a single stationary wheelchair, positioned for either forward or parallel approach.
verifier_note: Confirmed 30 in x 48 in clear floor space for wheelchairs in 521 CMR 6.4.

question: Under 521 CMR, when the clear floor space only allows a forward approach to an object, the maximum high forward reach permitted is:
explanation: Per 521 CMR 6.5, where only a forward approach is possible, the maximum high forward reach is 48 inches and the minimum low forward reach is 15 inches. (54 inches is the maximum high SIDE reach for a parallel approach.)
verifier_note: Confirmed 48 in max high forward reach / 15 in min low forward reach in 521 CMR 6.5. Distractor C (54 in) is intentionally the side-reach figure from 6.6.

question: Under 521 CMR, when the clear floor space allows a parallel approach by a person in a wheelchair, the maximum high side reach permitted is:
explanation: Per 521 CMR 6.6, where a parallel approach is allowed, the maximum high side reach is 54 inches and the low side reach must be no less than 9 inches above the floor. (48 inches is the forward-reach maximum.)
verifier_note: Confirmed 54 in max high side reach / 9 in min low side reach in 521 CMR 6.6. Distractor A (48 in) is the forward-reach figure from 6.5.

question: Under 521 CMR, the maximum slope allowed for an accessible ramp is:
explanation: Per 521 CMR 24.2, the maximum slope of a ramp is 1:12 (8.3%), with no tolerance allowed on slope. The maximum rise for any single run is 30 inches. (A steeper slope between 1:10 and 1:12 is allowed only for a single rise of a maximum of 3 inches.)
verifier_note: Confirmed 1:12 max ramp slope and 30 in max rise in 521 CMR 24.2.

question: Under 521 CMR, which of the following is correct regarding houses of worship?

options.A: Houses of worship are fully exempt from 521 CMR
options.B: Only new construction of houses of worship must comply; existing ones never do
options.C: Houses of worship (including churches, chapels, temples, synagogues, and mosques) must comply with 521 CMR except as modified in 521 CMR 16.00
options.D: Only the parking lot of a house of worship must be accessible

explanation: Per 521 CMR 16.1, houses of worship -- including but not limited to churches, chapels, temples, synagogues, and mosques -- must comply with 521 CMR except as specified or modified in 521 CMR 16.00. Areas open to the congregation, administrative offices, and religious meeting halls and classrooms must be accessible.
verifier_note: Confirmed house-of-worship scope and listed facility types in 521 CMR 16.1.

question: Under 521 CMR 19.00 (Recreational Facilities), which statement about courts (such as tennis, racquet, and squash courts) in a recreation facility is correct?

options.A: Courts are explicitly exempt from accessibility requirements
options.B: Areas open to and used by the public, including tennis, racquet, and squash courts, must be accessible
options.C: Only spectator seating must be accessible; the courts themselves never must be
options.D: Courts must comply only if the facility is owned by a municipality

explanation: Per 521 CMR 19.1, all areas open to and used by the public -- including locker rooms, shower facilities, weight rooms, and tennis, racquet, and squash courts -- must be accessible in recreation facilities. Recreational facilities must comply with 521 CMR except as modified in 521 CMR 19.00.
verifier_note: Confirmed in 521 CMR 19.1 that tennis/racquet/squash courts and other public areas must be accessible. NOTE: the source standard lists 'racquet and squash courts' generally and does NOT state a specific 'one of each type' numeric threshold; question is scoped to the verified general requirement to avoid asserting an unverified count.

question: Under 521 CMR, when drinking fountains (water bubblers) are provided, which statement is correct?

options.A: Drinking fountains are exempt from accessibility requirements
options.B: Where only one drinking fountain is provided on a floor, it must be accessible; where more than one is provided, one in each location must be accessible and on an accessible route
options.C: Only fountains in the lobby must be accessible
options.D: All drinking fountains may be mounted at any height the owner chooses

explanation: Under 521 CMR, where only one drinking fountain is provided on a floor it must be accessible; where more than one is provided on a floor, one in each location must be accessible and located on an accessible route. The general rule is that if a facility (toilet, telephone, drinking fountain) is provided, an accessible version must also be provided.
verifier_note: Confirmed the one-per-floor / one-per-location accessible drinking-fountain rule and the general 'if provided, must be accessible' principle tied to 521 CMR 3.3. Drinking fountains are chapter 36 per mass.gov 521cmr36.
